1.right:
Pashtun 2 June 1928

If any individual seeks to expand their shop and business,
they should advertise within the pages of *Pashtun*.

How to do so effectively:

*Pashtun* is a publication circulating throughout the border provinces, printed in vast quantities and reaching even the most remote towns. It is widely read by Pashtun men and women alike, serving as a vital source of information for the Pashtun people. It is considered a trusted and reliable voice throughout the world.  It is held in high esteem and is never discarded.

Why advertising should be conducted responsibly:

Advertising must be truthful, reflecting the nature of the goods or services being offered. It should not contravene religious or moral principles, nor should it unfairly exploit the caste, religion, or interests of others.  Furthermore, advertisements should be published in Pashto, and if translated from another language, should be rendered with clarity and precision.

How to place an advertisement:

You may submit your advertisement to the *Munjeer* (Editor) for consideration for inclusion in the advertising section of *Pashtun*.

June 1928, p. 4 Pashtun

Ghazal of Badshah Ahmad Shah Abdali

My heart was shattered by the sight of two beautiful women, a storm of longing has seized it.
For that reason, it remains restless, consumed by this turmoil.
This is not a fault of their beauty, but a fault of my own heart.
The number of my longing before you is a thousand times greater than my own.
If I counsel my heart, the pain will be unbearable.
I will face the world, and face it again, facing the world’s vastness.
When I stood upon the mountain of my strength, my heart was locked in its power.
This poem is born from the tears of my soul, a testament to your beauty.
What market could I find, today I stole your beauty away!
Hasan Baj, who did not kill him, held the world captive in his gaze.
I say I will not love, my heart is powerless without you.
It was not love that brought peace to my soul, only your presence.
My heart has no resolution, no rest until it beholds your beauty.
2.left:
The renowned saying in Pashtun culture is that the year of the ox is known from its gait.
Amir Ghazi, in all his words and deeds, if one were to carefully consider every aspect of his life, would surely understand that this man was created by God with a particular disposition and for a specific purpose. He did not acquire this love and affection for his people through mere trade or profit, but rather, God bestowed upon him this quality – a trait not born of Pashtun ignorance or animosity.  Any individuals within a tribe who have sacrificed their lives for two acres of land, and have betrayed their brothers for the sake of a small gain, will inevitably develop a sense of loyalty and affection for those who have stood by them.
Amir Sahib is of this tribe, and he is well aware of this, yet he remains deeply devoted to his people.  Allah has bestowed upon him this love, and it is evident. It remains unknown how this national love was achieved.

The tenth annual meeting of the Atmanzo Association “Islah al-Afaghna” was held. A sermon of welcome was delivered.
In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful – Praise be to Him, and peace and blessings be upon His Messenger.
But now, first I would like to express my gratitude to my esteemed guests, who have traveled a long and arduous road to attend this meeting. I offer my sincere thanks and express my appreciation for any kindnesses they have shown the association. The sympathy they have displayed is evident in the increased enthusiasm and dedication of the workers and staff. May Allah grant them the appropriate reward. Although the association has not provided services commensurate with the needs of its members, we still hold hope that it will not unduly burden them. For each person has their place. Another matter is that it is difficult to maintain a clear mind in the times of such factions. The Islah al-Afaghna Association is aware of this difficulty and has accounted for the total income, expenses, outstanding debts, and educational status of the previous year, which are presented for your consideration. Secondly, it is important that the citizens of this country – the residents of a nation – become familiar with their national rights, for the law of nature dictates that every soul has a natural inclination towards love of country and home. This is the inherent nature of every living being: to find peace and security in their own homeland, in their own home. It is a matter of necessity that each inhabitant of a land possesses the power to protect and defend their homeland, and it is a natural right. Looking back from the past to the present, we see that the protection and management of a country do not require small or petty fortifications, nor is there any need for a single test to pass an examination. Rather, a few strong and capable individuals must be gathered together as a center of leadership.

12.right:
Pashtun June 1928, 22

It is desired to protect the homeland from the influences of foreign powers and the passion for sacrifice, which is always present in the hearts of its inhabitants – this is the primary cause of its true success. During the conquest of Mecca, a decree came from the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, for the Muslims, especially for the refugees – it came in this particular manner: “Those who are exiled from you, remove them from your land” (Sahih al-Bukhari). Many such examples exist of the sacrifices made by the women of the Muslim faith. However, it is regrettable that in our time, such a proportion of sacrifice does not exist among us. If we were able to achieve the spirit of the Companions of the Prophet, we would certainly attain a glorious state, and our purpose would be fulfilled. It is just and fair that the Muslim world, in its entirety, has established equal rights for all people. Islam has decreed that all are equal, but it is known that Islam teaches that the true purpose of life is to promote the faith, and this is the meaning: “Indeed, Allah does not change a people until they change what is in themselves” (Quran 2:25). The true characteristics of a people – their courage, their sense of justice – cannot be altered until they themselves change those qualities. They must not forget their own rights and freedoms. Islam and the teachings of Islam are a gift from Almighty God to the world, and they are intended to establish the natural inclinations of man. All human beings are born with innate qualities, and these rights are inviolable and cannot be forcibly acquired.  Let us not forget this fundamental principle.

Regarding the session of the “Movement for the Reform of Afghanistan,” the address of welcome:

In the name of God, the Most Merciful, the Most Compassionate – We praise God and send blessings and peace upon His Messenger, Muhammad.  However, after a great deal of effort and hardship, I address you, my esteemed friends, who have come to this session after a long and difficult journey. I sincerely express my gratitude and appreciation for the efforts made in this movement, and I thank those who have shown empathy for the difficulties experienced by its members. While the movement has not been able to provide the highest level of service to its friends, I hope that its efforts will not be overlooked or undervalued.  Since the movement’s activities and expenditures for the past year have been clearly outlined, and since the interests of the residents of Afghanistan – the people of the country – have been brought to their attention, recognizing that the law of nature dictates that every human being seeks refuge and love in their homeland, and that every living being desires peace and security in their own home – it is impossible for any nation to protect and maintain its own territory without the strength and resources of its people.  It is not through small schemes or encroachments that a nation can achieve progress, but rather through the qualities of a few – courage and a central authority – that a nation can truly flourish.

June 1920, 25 Pashtun

As the Prophet said, “The nations will gather around you as the hungry gather around a loaf of bread” (Sahih al-Bukhari).  The Messenger of God, peace and blessings be upon him, when he migrated, he brought his trusted companion Ali to Medina (Sahih al-Bukhari).  He said, “If you wish, I will migrate, but may your head be lowered.” Ali replied, “I am ready to sacrifice my life for you, O Messenger of God.”  And so, the Prophet migrated, leaving his family and loved ones behind.  The Companions of the Prophet, may God be pleased with them, have sacrificed their lives for the propagation of the faith in the same way.  It is regrettable, however, that in our time, this is not the case.  Currently, the propagation of the faith is not even a priority.  However, it is known that God Almighty has decreed that the Muslim Ummah is the best community brought forth for mankind, commanding good and forbidding evil.  Men have a higher status than women, but Islam has established equal rights for all.  Indeed, the most honorable among you before God are those who are most righteous.  The Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, has given an example: “Whoever is among you desires to be closer to Allah, let him spend what he earns” (Quran 92:3).  The Messenger of God said, “The love of the world and the hatred of death are the cause of all evil.”  This is the reason why the south Indian movement for progress fell apart.  Let us now focus on the work that must be done to achieve progress in this world, and let us find the spirit of sacrifice within ourselves, and for this, may the Almighty guide us.

17.right:
Pashtun
32
Pashtun Reform
Abdul Rab Khan Saheb Fakhr. B.A., LL.B. (Alik Vakil, Peshawar)

Initially, the British administration or the region of the Frontier established a system of governance and began to close temporary dams, followed by legal prisons in 170-174 and then another in 196-197. In addition to these restrictions, a significant undertaking was undertaken to gather information regarding all tribal lands, both hereditary and spiritual, and to impose restrictions on their transactions. This was done with the unfortunate result of imposing restrictions on all tribes, repeatedly expressing the grievances of the Pashtun people in their language (Na'u'd by Allah min zalik) in these transactions.  This injustice, inflicted upon them, provoked a terrible reaction, and God’s punishment was sought for their actions.

Islam has accomplished many great deeds throughout the world, and one of its most significant achievements is the establishment of laws to protect women from oppression, which was rampant before its arrival.  Women were treated as mere possessions, stripped of their human status, but Islam enacted numerous laws to elevate them to the same level as men.  This resulted in the widespread expression of these grievances in the Pashtun lands, repeatedly documented in their customs and traditions.  These practices, reflecting a disregard for the Sharia, were frequently lamented and condemned.  The Pashtun man, consumed by honor and shame, would often find himself in a state of despair, his grief manifesting as a devastating flood, leaving him bewildered and ashamed of his own helplessness.  

Let me, as a leader and brother, address you, my fellow Pashtuns, and express my willingness to submit to the guidance of a wise and just leader, should one arise.  Yes, we are the oppressed, the picture of misery, and it is our duty to seek redress through the Sharia.

19.right:
Pashtun 37 June 1928

Alone in the world, let us turn towards no one save ourselves! (A lament)
My heart aches – there is no balm for this wound.
My face is a mask of sorrow, a wretched image of man.
The shadow of my father’s house weighs heavily upon me – a drowning fate.
I am consumed by anxiety, devoid of the joy of childhood.
1. O Allah! There is no one like us in this world, burdened as we are.
Grant us your mercy and kindness, O door of salvation for the oppressed and the destitute.
It is not known to us the extent of our failings – yet your grace extends even to those who oppress us.
Your wrath does not stir, your anger remains dormant.
When, then, will our hearts, so burdened with sorrow, finally find release?
Let us not be consumed by despair, let us not be overwhelmed by the weight of our suffering.
(They) yearn for your grace, and fear your wrath.
Let us cry out, “Save us!”
May I, on the day of Judgment, lament my fate.
Then, in the hereafter, my heart will be laid bare.
I seek refuge in God, for how can I conceal my grief?
Why does my heart yearn for solace?
Look down upon the plight of the helpless.
No one but you can be my refuge.
2. Let me weep, let me reveal the secret of my own despair.
The world has turned its back on me, my family is estranged, my brothers are distant.
They have passed me by, blind to my suffering.
O great God, what do I do?  What is my fate?

All: “Yes, yes, certainly, certainly!”
Leaders! This is the story of the oppressed, the images of their suffering that reside within your heart.

Pashtun

Also appeared. A salutation to the District Commissioner upon presentation is not out of self-control or courtesy. Consequently, we resolved that greetings are a mark of respect. There is no difference between our independence and a simple Salaam. Greetings are, indeed, required. For it is our goal to find individuals of good character and high morals. Upon arriving at the District Commissioner’s bungalow, I first heard Okara. I did not proceed to the police station. Instead, I found myself seated on a black and white chair, a solitary figure. A greeting was offered, and I noticed a dark shadow by the river valley. Mr. Orboz, observing me at times with a troubled expression and at others scrutinizing his own dark face, called out to me. He had journeyed to Kabul, and the government granted him permission to return to the country, though he did not accomplish anything. Yet, he was relieved of his sorrow. The player expressed his displeasure, addressing me with a sharp rebuke. He inquired as to how I had returned home. I explained that this was also a matter of concern for our nation, O Dalam. Regardless of where my heart resides, I would return to this land. I implore you, do not burden me with further concerns. Later, Mr. Sahib’s anger became palpable. He turned his face dark with displeasure. He demanded that I follow a particular example.  He repeated the same request several times. (more on that)

31.right:
January 1929

Education in Afghanistan

A Period of Development

Various subjects pertaining to Afghanistan’s diverse fields are regularly published in the *Sarmal* newspaper each week, and I believe these offer valuable insights into the current era. I would like to express my appreciation for the guidance of Amanullah Khan, whose efforts are proving beneficial, and to share with you news of progress – though I hesitate to boast excessively. My sincere wishes for success in your endeavors. However, the most reliable measure of intention and effort is the dedication of resources to education. Therefore, Afghanistan’s Shah is justly regarded as the most commendable among all tribes.

Pashtun
10

The Simon Commission

There is unrest throughout the country, with the people gathered in groups. Some claim that our salvation lies within the Commission, while others argue that it will merely leave our country without offering a solution. A Commission was dispatched to Mobah Saran, receiving a warm welcome, and a boycott was presented as a demonstration of loyalty to our people. The matter of reception and boycott will be addressed through reasoned discussion and consensus. Despite the constant noise of the river, we must not allow past grievances to cloud our judgment. Let us remember Ghazi Amanullah and the Commission’s reports, rather than dwelling on past disputes. The Loya Jirga (Parliament) should be entrusted with the welfare of the people. I will elaborate on this matter in a later section. It is not the duty of the British to provide medicine to the Afghan people; rather, it is their responsibility to uphold justice and fairness. Let the grievances of the common people be addressed through proper channels and resolutions, and let the issues of reception and boycott be resolved amicably. Let us put an end to the endless speculation and uncertainty.

Islamia College and the Literary Circle

(I, Fazl Haq Sheed Mahmood Abadi, a student at Islamia College, am pleased to announce the establishment of the “Bazm-e-Adab” (Literary Circle) on November 29th. May God be praised that our young people are embracing their own language with enthusiasm and dedication. I extend my congratulations to the Principal of the College and wish them continued success in their noble endeavors. – More Love)

Method of Education

Education is offered through various schools and seminaries. The traditional curriculum is divided into three stages: *Ibtil Shieh* (the foundational stage), *Rashid Yeh* (the growth stage), and *Aali Dih* (the advanced stage).
31.left:
Pashtun
12
Various schools exist for different sciences, and they are named accordingly. Here is a description of each:

*   **Telegram Office:** Work related to telegrams is conducted here. (Electric Telegraph)
*   **Map Office:** The work of drawing maps of countries is done here.
*   **Agriculture Office:** The science of agriculture is taught here…
*   **Trade Office:** Work related to trade is conducted here…
*   **Metal Office:** Various languages are taught here, such as Turkish, French, English, and others…
*   **Government Office:** The art of governance, administration, and the interpretation of laws is taught here, though it is not always successful. Then the work of the government is carried out. This means that governors are appointed. The principles of the British government are a good example. The branch of the school is…
*   **Principal’s Office:** This is a school for administrators. It teaches the art of administration. It is in accordance with the *Ting Kalam* (the spirit of the nation). The Darul Uloom Arabiya (Arabic Seminary) teaches religious studies, philosophy, and other subjects.
*   **Military Office:** Military training is imparted here, and all work related to the army is conducted here.

This is a list of all the schools for each science. In addition to these, there are also a few smaller institutions for general education. They are…

**The College of Habibullah Khan (deceased)**

This college, established in memory of Amir Habibullah Khan, offers instruction in geography, history, literature, mathematics, and theology. Turkish and English are also taught here. These are the established levels.

**The Abadi College:** This college is also established in the same manner. The method of education is also the same. Only here, German language instruction is given in particular.

There are three hundred schools in Afghanistan and another fifty are being established. All students in Afghanistan are currently enrolled in schools. Unfortunately, other nations do not provide the same level of education. Only the children of the wealthy are afforded this opportunity. Therefore, it is hoped that the Shah of Afghanistan will provide education freely to all.  The Afghan people are considered the most advanced in the world in terms of education, meaning that all people will become literate and able to read and write. In schools for ten years, the Afghan people will be in opposition to the half-educated Indian people, a people known for their civilization and knowledge of science and government, living under the rule of a government with a high level of progress. Provided that the enemies’ malicious schemes and oppression are overcome.

(The above is a sincere expression of my hope.)

It is thus understood that the people of the Pashtun nation have, for generations, borne the burden of service, sacrificing their own well-being for the preservation of their community. The deeds of many remarkable women in history attest to this dedication – women whose efforts were paramount to the survival of the Pashtun people. While I will not delve into exhaustive detail, I will mention a few of these exemplary figures, whose courage and devotion are worthy of remembrance. It is a simple truth that this perception of our people has shifted. We once believed that only through self-sacrifice could the nation endure.

I state this not as a new realization, but as a correction to a long-held misconception. It is not merely for personal desires that we seek fulfillment, but for the service of our people. The demands of the present moment far outweigh any individual aspirations, and we must therefore fulfill our duties accordingly. It is not something that will be apparent to you. Indeed, only through selfless service can we truly benefit the nation. This service extends beyond the battlefield and into the realm of ‘jihad’ and ‘aman’ – both in times of war and peace. Should we succumb to weakness, our children would likewise be weak. If we embrace a spirit of betrayal, our offspring will inherit that same treacherous path. Therefore, no son should ever sacrifice himself for the sake of his people or his country. Within this piece, I wish to express my desire for the progress of Afghanistan in terms of civilization, education, and justice – all of which are currently prevalent in the West (Europe).

Let us not be swayed by fleeting pleasures, nor lose ourselves in worldly pursuits.
Let us remember that the true path lies in piety and righteousness.
He will not be swayed by the temptations of the world.
“Yes, yes, this is the truth.” Let us not be ashamed to seek honor and respect.
Let us not be afraid to speak the truth, nor to defend our dignity.
Let us not be blinded by wealth and power, nor lose our way in the pursuit of worldly gain.
Let us always strive to do what is right, and to uphold the values of our ancestors.
Let us not be swayed by the opinions of others, nor to abandon our own convictions.
Let us always be true to ourselves, and to the principles we hold dear.

44.right:
January 1929

Each daily meeting, whatever its purpose, was fulfilled. Now, Flournelli’s companionship will be realized – a joyous assembly concluding this evening. Saeed, in his heart, was filled with astonishment every hour – “O God, if I am severed from these further misfortunes, will I not also rise?” He was lost in thought, Flournelli saying, “The Citadel of Al-Hamra is where I am.” Saeed raised his eyes, and exclaimed, “Allahu Akbar! How magnificent is this great fortress! It is evident that the sky stretches above its high walls, and the towers, reaching towards the heavens, are positioned in rows along the windows. The head and foot, laid upon the earth’s floor, appear strangely white.” He mused, “One might imagine a diverse array of flowers blooming in the gardens of paradise.” Saeed observed the scale of the construction, and the grandeur of its architect – a balm for the foot’s misfortune. And this verse: -(Arabic poetry)
“And I stood at Al-Hamra, a pilgrim and a tomb, a collection of diverse souls.”

Then he spoke, “O Hamra, is there a return?” And the fortress replied, “And does he return who has died?”
“Indeed, the era consumes all things swiftly; alas, it does not return.”
Therefore, let us examine their memories – let us read their inscriptions! It is a pity that my weeping and lamentations have yielded no benefit; for the spirits of those who have passed are like radiant stars, recalling a diverse assembly.

Saeed now found himself before a desolate hearth. He observed the floor beneath its corner, a series of rooms stretching along its sides, seemingly conversing with the sky. Saeed pondered, imagining that he might have resided there in the past, or perhaps, his gaze involuntarily overflowed with sorrow, drawn by the sight of the floor’s exposed beams.  He felt a longing, a desire to dwell within that space, to experience a moment of respite, or to gaze upon the sky from its lofty heights.  But Florenli’s school did not find this melancholy fitting.  Soon after, it was heard that Florenli was deeply engrossed in the detailed drawings of the building’s architecture.  He therefore proceeded to the hidden, inner chambers, considering the fate of the arched doorway – a matter of considerable deliberation.  Each time he reached the bed, he would glance at the inscription affixed to the door, and it would strike him with a sudden, overwhelming feeling.  Then the voice of Yabta (O father-in-law) would escape his lips, and he would fall unconscious, overwhelmed by a multitude of sensations.

Then he spoke, “O Hamra, is there a return?” And the fortress replied, “And does he return who has died?”

About the people in Afghanistan, which has been published, in fact, it is for the people who are trying for freedom in India.

50.right:
January 1920

It is said that in Afghanistan, regarding the news circulating about the people of Hindustan, some claim it is largely unfounded. They say that the Muslims of Hindustan, in light of what has transpired in Afghanistan, are being portrayed in a manner that is deeply misleading. We understand that the people of Hindustan, under the oppression of their governments at home, do not fully grasp how their affairs are viewed in Afghanistan. There, they are treated with great kindness, particularly by those striving for the freedom of Hindustan. The news circulating about the people of Afghanistan, in truth, is for those seeking liberation for the Muslims of Hindustan. It is vitally important that the people of Hindustan be treated with kindness in Afghanistan, so that they may pursue their aspirations for independence. We understand that this news is, in essence, for those in Hindustan who are working towards freedom. It is not unfounded that the people of Hindustan seek independence in Afghanistan.
50.left:
January 1920

The world is consumed by turmoil, and it seems that Muslims are becoming increasingly pessimistic. If what is said is true, then it is only a matter of time before this becomes a reality. Regarding Afghanistan, when news reaches us of the English exploiting Muslims with force, demanding they pay exorbitant taxes, forcing them to work without pay, confining them to their homes, suppressing gatherings, and destroying religious assemblies, the minds of the Muslims become clouded. Even their reason falters. Khan, however, avoids speaking about such matters; he is past his prime. It is clear that this habit has its roots in a long-standing custom. Indeed, it is a proverb that a donkey that eats grass will destroy the fodder.

This is also the current proverb, and it is not being ignored. News has reached us that the order to worship God in the Turkestan region has been banned, and the instructions to desecrate holy places have been outlawed under the government of Hindustan. Then, in a short time, the price of merchandise in the market has risen dramatically. It is a strange and unprecedented occurrence.  The news is circulating that the Governor, Mr. Dost Mohammad Khan, is now a prisoner of the British, and that the King’s relations with Afghanistan are strained.  The King has not visited Jallabad, nor has his envoy been sent there, nor has he issued any decrees for the region.  Nor has the King been relieved of his duties to the Bakhtiar province.  Indeed, the Afghan Consul General, Mr. General, who is stationed in the bosom of the Persian Queen, reports that this news is spreading through the newspapers of the British Empire, which are filled with such falsehoods. Some say that certain people claim that in Afghanistan, the news circulating about the people of Hindustan is largely unfounded. They say that the Muslims of Hindustan, under the oppression of their governments at home, do not fully grasp how their affairs are viewed in Afghanistan. There, they are treated with great kindness, particularly by those striving for the freedom of Hindustan. The news circulating about the people of Afghanistan, in truth, is for those seeking liberation for the Muslims of Hindustan. It is not unfounded that the people of Hindustan seek independence in Afghanistan.

51.right:
According to accounts from this period of Pashto, a king ruled with a particular concern for his people. He directed considerable thought and effort towards them, undertaking numerous actions in the name of religion. It was observed that this king held a deep affection for his subjects, and that he invested heavily in their well-being, often expressing his sentiments through public pronouncements.  His aim was to ensure the service of his people to their faith, a devotion that extended to every aspect of their lives.  Muslims, it was believed, should strive to uphold their religious obligations, and the name of God should be invoked in all endeavors.  The king’s advisors, including figures like Sheikh Moharim, were deeply involved in matters pertaining to the Persian Gulf, overseeing the administration of the region and seeking to maintain stability.  Furthermore, the interests of Afghanistan and Persia were closely intertwined, and the king’s efforts were focused on fostering amicable relations between the two nations.  News arrived from Russia and the East, concerning the ascension of His Highness Shah Reza Shah, prompting a sense of anticipation and hope.  Finally, it was learned that Sheikh Moharim’s interests extended to the Persian Gulf, where his influence was firmly established, and the effects of his actions were felt throughout the region.  The king’s governor, Dost Mohammad Khan, was highly regarded, and the arrival of Shah Reza was met with great enthusiasm.  The relationship between Afghanistan and Iran was also well-established, and both countries maintained a close connection.  Should any opportunities arise, as suggested by the counsel of Gazi Mokal Kamel, the king might consider ventures into Kurdistan, where the affairs of the region were overseen by the esteemed Sardar Keyl.  The king’s commitment to his people was unwavering, and he sought to maintain their loyalty and prosperity.  He was known for his generosity and his dedication to the welfare of his subjects.

55.right:
Pashtun
January 1920

Kingald served as the Vice-Chancellor at the time. In a previous era, he was known for accepting bribes, but he vehemently opposed such practices. At the same time, he refused to participate in the distribution of bribes. This steadfastness was a testament to his integrity. The reason for his resistance stemmed from the corrupt nature of the marketplace, where such dealings were commonplace. He responded to accusations of bribery with a firm declaration: “A true leader does not engage in such deceit.” He further stated, “This is a new order, and without bribery, no work can be accomplished.” He then added, “The burden of leadership rests upon my shoulders, and I will not allow my clan to suffer for my sake.” He asserted, “If I am to be held accountable, let it be for the good of my people.”

He continued, “It is said that the government is a burden, but I will not allow it to become a source of hardship for my people.” He then stated, “If I were to be imprisoned for the sake of my clan, I would rather face that fate than see my people suffer.”

He was then questioned: “What is the purpose of the King’s authority?” He replied, “Without bribery, no work can be done. And I speak the truth.” He then added, “This is a new order, and without bribery, how can it be achieved?” He paused, considering his words carefully. “It is said that the burden of the kingdom rests upon my shoulders, and I will not allow my clan to suffer for my sake.”

In response, he stated, “If I am to be held accountable, let it be for the good of my people.”

He then said, “It is said that the government is a burden, but I will not allow it to become a source of hardship for my people.” He continued, “If I were to be imprisoned for the sake of my clan, I would rather face that fate than see my people suffer.”

A messenger arrived, informing him of his impending arrest. His jailer also arrived. The jailer was a devout Muslim, but a rogue, and impoverished. The messenger, a haughty Ferangi, spoke with disdain about Kingald’s court. However, he was unfamiliar with the English language. He believed that a prisoner’s life in jail was a miserable existence. Kingald’s lawyer arrived later, and pleaded with him. “Look! The King is suffering. Kingald has been reined in. He will pay for it. And they have not paid.” He continued, “You will be locked in this room for the rest of your life, and Michan will come out.”

He cried out, “If death is dear, self-death is a failure.” Kingald understood this. He was born into the movement, so he was brought out like a grain of mustard seed. It was a difficult task. This would happen every day.

I gave thanks with great joy that day. I was wrong, so I consoled myself. It takes effort. Maqe Zam and Tule, Michan to and Radiyam. and Dham started. The match does not work - and what will happen?

Look at one of the shakurs, and the warkhali abe came running. What and hey! I will be grateful. I offer my condolences from the bottom of my heart. I took it in May. A prisoner came to the dacoit. Eight and seven. That you will not forget it. We raised the source of support a little. Walk me.

Showed Poke it in a way. After that, he left. I was suffering from the ninth labor. The whole of this crime is in the heart of Mazigro. Bakken crossed my arms. Today Jamdan came, by the labor, and he laughed. And Khan with him.

15

January 1920

Dev Mulla Sahib

A question:

1.  Disregard the legends of the Qur’an.
2.  Ascend to the inheritance of the Prophet.
3.  Do not bring forth matters of blame upon others.
4.  The heart is always in need of sustenance.
5.  When life is prosperous, the stomach is full.
6.  Worship is permissible in Islam when one is not preoccupied.
7.  Mosques and religious schools are always found.
8.  Listen to the accounts of the world’s disbelief and the Islam of the Fergies.

Those who were burdened with the Torah, and did not carry it as an ox carries bundles of scrolls.

Or, regarding the honor of the scholars – it is not that they sought the meaning of the Holy Qur’an, but rather that the Pashtuns are not like them.  The Khan, the elder, the Syed, those in the valleys and the plains – all are like this.  Let us not speak of their faults, nor should we consider them to be on the right path.  Let us strive to bring our Pashtun nation and our people to the proper path, and let the messages and news from our land be intended for the benefit and purpose of the world.  It is a great thing that the names of Mullahs appear in these publications – let us not cause confusion, let us not insult the honor of the people, and let us not consider that the Pashtuns are subservient to the ignorant.  The Pashtuns are loyal to the righteous and to God.  A further point is that some believe that we are self-sufficient, and that Pashtuns will not be included in the affairs of others.  Other topics will be included in these publications.

Certainly, this is a mistake. The Pashtun is the Pashtun of the Pashtuns.  Anyone who brings a subject to the fore will be included.  But it is necessary that the subject be included.  Now, Mullah and Khan Sahibs have written to the Pashtun – let us first examine their claims.  Let us consider the merits of the Pashtun and the people of this region, and let us strive to bring them to the right path.  The news and publications from our land are intended for the benefit and purpose of the world.  And this is the matter – why do the names of Mullahs appear in these publications?  Let us not cause confusion, let us not insult the honor of the people, and let us not consider that the Pashtuns are subservient to the ignorant.  The Pashtuns are loyal to the righteous and to God.  A further point is that some believe that we are self-sufficient, and that Pashtuns will not be included in the affairs of others.  Other topics will be included in these publications.

Certainly, this is a mistake. The Pashtun is the Pashtun of the Pashtuns.  Anyone who brings a subject to the fore will be included.  But it is necessary that the subject be included.  Now, Mullah and Khan Sahibs have written to the Pashtun – let us first examine their claims.

Let us consider the matter of the King – information for the reform of the nation – is the defense of the nation’s honor, and its prosperity, and its security.  (Abdul Gafar)

Be fair – who establishes the standard of a nation’s greatness? Let us remember God. Langar, sustained by money, now that the British possess funds, we do not. Careful consideration is required, for our people, stripped of their faith and clad in foreign garments, are being diminished and destroyed. A non-Muslim, feigning adherence to Islam, will arrive. He will not be considered a Sheikh, but a mere pretender. Wealth and possessions will be showered upon him, and he will disappear, seeking refuge elsewhere.

Let us cast aside lengthy pronouncements and consider the present day. The kingdom of the Turks was shattered by internal strife. The Afghan Sultanate, a brief and disastrous experiment (Blessed be God). This misfortune has befallen us Muslims, a consequence of the Widow Lawrence (known in the West as the Widow Lawrence), and presently, we cannot discern what fate awaits us.

In the service of the Pashtun people, generally, in the service of religious scholars, Sayyids, and elders, we offer this humble plea: a small contribution can resolve divisions between religion and politics. Yet, still, we hold onto hope that this nation may be spared destruction. May this debt be repaid. Let us invoke the names of Islam and the peaceful rulers. In this state, we bear this responsibility—

Mahmoud Jirga’s Two Representatives (Karzai)

It is necessary to state these matters first. What is the purpose and meaning of the Mahmoud Jirga? Why is this information withheld?  It is generally understood that this is for the imagination of the people – there is no need for this meeting. Everyone acknowledges this. Let us not name individuals without a letter or a formal designation; it is considered an extravagance.  As in past times, in provinces throughout the world, Congresses and Caliphates maintain these associations. However, many people remain outside their influence, and the Muslims of other regions do not even consider them.  They believe that these individuals will bring about reforms for the people of Hindustan. And they claim to have taken action towards this goal, and to have obtained certain privileges.  The situation is as follows: the Viceroy of Hindustan, this is our predicament. The government is considering what to do regarding the people of Hindustan and their rights. And this is our situation – we are awaiting developments.  If we are to be judged, we are in a state of uncertainty.

Regarding the past, the Khyber region has little connection to these matters.  On the other hand, the people of Hindustan believe that the Caliphate is a religious matter of Khyber.  Other people are not concerned with it, besides the Hindus and Sikhs.  It is said that the Caliphate has no need for power, only for faith.  The Pashtuns, however, are not involved in the Congress or the Caliphate, only in matters of faith.  If Hazrat Umer (may God have mercy on him) had acted in this manner, it would have been a wise course.  Many Pashtuns harbor suspicions regarding the Congress and the Caliphate’s true intentions. This is undeniable. They believe that these powers are seeking to divide the people. The result of this is that the Congress and the Caliphate are not drawing people to themselves.  It is as if the Pashtuns are saying that Hazrat Umer and his companions were the ideal rulers.  Let us not be involved in such endeavors.  Let us consider the situation of our people, and let us not engage in these matters.  If the Congress is a committee, or if the Caliphate is a…

63.right:
The situation in Hindustan is developing, as it appears now, and through this committee, the people of this province are told that Muslims, or Sikhs, are sacrificing themselves for the people of India. They received cooperation from the other side – specifically, a gift of thousands of rupees. Consequently, there is more cooperation in the border region than anywhere else, but in reality, India profits from this, and those who have made no effort towards it claim to have brought reforms to India and possess legitimate authority. This discovery has led to the announcement of various reforms (Dominion) to some of the victims. I observe that the Viceroy of India is aware of our situation at this time. The government considers what is best, and does not favor any house other than India. This is the state of our suffering – that yesterday, in our Gwad (Afghanistan), and among the English (British) and some others, we sought refuge in those events. And because of this, the people of our border are divided, with money brought from India to meet their needs. Presently, there are hundreds of rupees available. Consequently, since we do not have a Jirga of our own, we are reliant on the generosity of others.

In Khyber, there is little connection with other classes. On the other hand, the people of India believe that the Caliphate is a religious matter for Muslims. What happens to some of these people – Hindus and Sikhs – besides the fact that there are no Muslims? They claim that the Caliphate requires something of all, and that the greatest benefit for all is the needy people of the Caliphate, who understand the religious aspects of this Caliphate. The Pashtuns, however, do not understand the Congress or the Caliphate. If dear heir were to try, it would be costly. In the hearts of some Pashtuns, there is a great conflict between the Congress and the true purpose of the Khilafat. There is no doubt in their hearts. In this assumption, what has been done by India. The result of this matter is that the Congress and the Khilafat are not close to these people or this tribe. It is clear that these people have constructed something for India. This matter has resulted in the Congress and the Khilafat not being close to these people or this tribe. If the Pashtuns say that the heirs and Qamul have built a Jirga that is used in such works, they do not know about each other’s situation, because our people have their own Jirga. And we have nothing. Others are preparing to work for it. What congress or civil work do you want the word of caliphate. For this, it became clear that the People’s Assembly is not for the Congress or the Caliphate, but rather for the strengthening of it, in which the people outside the Congress and the Caliphate are included. 64.right:
January 1928

I have previously informed my closest friends that our monthly *Pashtun Risala* does not receive subscribers, nor are returned copies sent back.  Furthermore, the magazine rarely reaches its readers, and some even fail to understand Pashto.  However, my associates have not agreed with this arrangement, which I consider a matter of commerce.  I now write to express my concern regarding a delay in receiving a copy of the *Pashtun Risala*, and to request a resolution.

Letter

To the Service of Mr. Abdul Ghaffar Khan

May this letter find you well. I understand from your recent writing that many of our brothers do not receive the *Pashtun Risala*.  I now submit a formal complaint: I have yet to receive a copy of the magazine this month.  We have consistently sent each copy by post, attached to every letter, and in duplicate – two copies per letter – but this has not been sufficient.  I was not particularly troubled by this until I recently examined the December issue in the shop of Hakeem Abdul Rahim.  Therefore, I offer my sincere apologies and request that I be promptly sent a true copy of the magazine.  Should this require an increased price, I will gladly pay it.  However, if it is not possible to send a copy at all, please do not send it through any other means, nor delay its arrival, lest it be delivered to the *Pashtun Akhwan* – the esteemed Mr. Maulvi Sahib of Waziristan – and the Pashtun engineers serving under him, nor to Reddy Pashtun Sahib of Waziristan.  I await your response with anticipation, inquiring whether you will provide me with the necessary Pashto and Pashtu translations, and if you are committed to the welfare of our people.

(Abdul Ghaffar)
Pashtun

And beyond the world?

O, Pashtun men! Do you understand that mere employment or other engagements, without genuine connection, do not lead to advancement.  We have come to the homes of our relatives, and what have we done for the people of our tribe? (Abdul Ghaffar)  Our banners and agreements will not be honored.

Pashtun and Political Agent

It is well known that our Pashtuns are in a particular state of affairs.  News and magazines of their tribes, disseminated through correspondence, foster agreement and progress among those nations, and inform them of the events and circumstances of the new era.

However, our pioneering Pashto publication, the *Pashtun* newspaper and magazine, is not distributed in this manner – this monthly magazine is not sent to the righteous people, nor does it reach them through the machinations of the oppressor.  Consequently, the people are disheartened and lose hope.  It is the imagination of the people that these small matters, roads, railways, and the expenses of books, are maintained for the peace and benefit of the people.

Yet, experience has shown that this is incorrect; these are being used to serve the interests of this king.  In this, their own purpose and benefit are paramount.

65.right:
January 1929

In this meeting, Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan, Abdul Akbar Khan, Rustam Khan, and other Pashtuns participated in this Jirga, which started in November. In this meeting, it was decided to form a "Zalmo Jirga" of Pashtuns, similar to the "All India Youth League" group in India. The main purpose of establishing this Jirga was to show Pashtuns the way to fight for national unity, independence and their rights. In this meeting, Pashtun leaders emphasized in their speeches that Pashtuns should take the path of unity and strive for a common goal. The leaders of this Jirga told the participants that without unity, Pashtuns cannot get their rights and for this purpose they should support each other in any situation. Similarly, in this meeting, decisions were made to strengthen the cultural and social affairs of Pashtuns. It is worth mentioning that the message of unity was spread in the meetings of this Jirga and the Pashtuns were instructed to rise as a united force. In the course of the work of this Jirga, the need to increase efforts for the unity and consensus among the youth in different areas of Pakhtunkhwa was mentioned.
65.left:
Pashtun 29

If he doesn't know Pashtun, interest and loss seem good. And if someone is fully aware of this, then he should look at it a little more carefully. For this reason, it is necessary that the people of the border province once again have a lot of knowledge about this village. In India, which is this group, which is called (All India Youth League), compared to our Zalmo Jirga of Pashtuns, there are many special differences in the culture and politics of Pashtuns. We know that this is a very important task for Pashtuns, that any Jirga, which is a Pashtun province, will be able to discuss and consult with the people of Pakhtunkhwa about the politics of Pashtuns and their demands. At this time, when Pashtuns take this responsibility for their unity, it is clearly a sign of great pride. For the creation of this group, a new and strong unity is needed for the Pashtuns at this time. There is a wide participation of the people in the establishment of this Jirga, and the contribution of each person in it is acceptable as a national duty. In the construction of this Jirga, it works as a foundation in which a new phase is taken for the future of Pashtuns. In September 1929, it was decided to establish such a Jirga for the people of the border province, and in October it was decided to hold a meeting for the establishment of the Central Jirga, and the decision of such a form of this Jirga is in use in the province.

66.right:
There reside in Bonn such noble people – those who serve their nation.
If anyone does not do so, the inevitable result is awakening within the community.
On November 23rd, Bando Abdul Akbar Khan, the head of the Jirga, and the moderator,
along with Lala Bahadur Khan and Pera Ismail Khan, convened – and
Abdul Qafar Khan, representing Farang and seeking a righteous path – all of us gathered on November 25th
at the Tank. Pera Ismail Khan held a meeting on November 26th –
Sardar Muhammad Aurangzeb Khan, a representative of the P.E.L., and Muhammad Ramzan Khan, a representative of the M.A.L.P., were also present – and
speeches were delivered, resulting in a significant political awakening in Dera Ismail Khan, far exceeding our initial assessment.
There were few attendees at the meeting; it falls to the organizers to make greater efforts and cultivate knowledgeable and working people within the community, safeguarding the nation’s interests.  It functions as a foundation, paving a new path for the Pashtun future.  In September 1929, a decision was made to establish a Jirga for the people of the Province of سرحد, and in October, a meeting was scheduled to establish a central Jirga, with a plan for a structure to be implemented throughout the province.

67.right:
January 1920

He possessed a fervent motivation, driven by a desire to honor his lineage. These men delighted in exchanging laughter and good cheer with one another. Mr. Bahadur, it seems, was known for his generosity, traveling from this district to other villages and paying seven rupees and ten rupees per house.

Furthermore, the people of these villages often regarded travelers with suspicion, refusing them hospitality. It is their ancestral homeland, a sacred space, and they would not simply offer it to anyone. They demand payment for its use, a practice the travelers themselves must accept. The people, however, have repeatedly voiced their grievances to the authorities, expressing their dissatisfaction with this arrangement. It is through this collective voice that we, the people, discern the true state of affairs. If this official system were to extend its reach to us, we would be forced to seek the counsel of a wise and respected elder, lending our meager resources to support our impoverished community.

On November 8th, the women of the region paid their respects to education. Along the road, near the village of Bo Clay, they offered their heartfelt devotion. We were astonished to witness such profound loyalty to their Pashtun heritage. Consequently, they convened a meeting in Banun, gathering Hindus, Muslims, and Sikhs. The atmosphere was particularly uplifting, as professionals, lawyers, and elders expressed their joy at contributing to the well-being of their community.

On November 23rd, we did not conclude our efforts at the Jama Masjid. Instead, we held a grand assembly, attended by thousands of people. Speeches were delivered by Lala Khan, elucidating the purpose of the gathering. The outpouring of love and sincerity displayed by the gentlemen of Banun’s legal profession was truly remarkable.
67.left:
Pashtun
30

Within Banun, a diverse and honorable populace resides, all striving to serve their nation. Consequently, the inevitable outcome is the rise of awareness throughout the community. On November 23rd, Mrs. Abdul Kabir Khan presided over the Jirga, and Mr. Lala Ismail Khan served as the moderator. Mr. Lala Bahadur Khan and Mr. Abdul Qafar Khan joined the proceedings, along with Mr. Meki. We had gathered in Tank on November 5th.

Amidst the camaraderie, we enjoyed meetings with many friends in Tank, sharing mutual affection and respect with lawyers and gentlemen. In addition to this, we often contemplated the future.

---

On November 28th, we journeyed to Banun, encountering the people of the region along the way. Their profound love and loyalty to their Pashtun heritage were evident.  We convened a meeting in Banun, gathering Hindus, Muslims, and Sikhs. The atmosphere was particularly vibrant, as professionals, lawyers, and elders expressed their joy at contributing to the well-being of their community.  We then proceeded to the meeting, where we conveyed the message of our elders to all present. With sincere hearts, we expressed our gratitude and esteem.  At approximately 6:00 PM, we held a meeting in Banun.  Again, Hindus, Muslims, and Sikhs were present. The space was filled with warmth and goodwill. It was a joyous occasion, as professionals, lawyers, and elders celebrated their shared heritage.

---

On November 25th, a large gathering was held in Tank, attended by numerous residents of the region.  Esteemed tribal leaders were also present.  During the meeting, we conveyed the message of our elders to all, expressing our gratitude and esteem.  On December 12th, a gathering was held in Lech, with participants also traveling from Kohat.  The organizers had informed the local community of the event.  Prayers were offered and requests were made.

---

On December 8th, we resumed our journey, concluding our tour of the regions of Baderg, Aeru, known throughout the area, Koht, Meena, Koht, and Spin.  It is well-known that these regions are rich in resources.  There is no doubt that the people are prepared, and the work of some laborers is being undertaken.  The goods are being sent to these regions, and it is a matter of record that the people are eager to receive them.  The wealthy landowners of the region have generously provided the necessary supplies.  The people have repeatedly expressed their gratitude to these landowners, and they continue to do so.  If these landowners were to provide assistance, it would be a great boon to the people.  However, if they were to deny assistance, it would be a great hardship.  The people of these mountainous regions have long suffered under the weight of the government’s demands, and they are now demanding a fairer share.  It is not uncommon for the people to be forced to sell their possessions to meet the government’s demands.  They are often subjected to unjust taxes and fees.  It is a grave injustice that the government does not recognize the rights of these people.  Furthermore, it is a grave injustice that non-Pashtuns are allowed to hunt and graze in these areas, while the Pashtun people are denied the same freedoms.  The elders of these tribes implore the government to protect the rights of their people, to ensure their freedom, prosperity, and honor, and to prevent them from being exploited by others.  Let the government not force them to sell their possessions to strangers, nor to deprive them of their ancestral lands.  Let the government ensure that the tribes are governed by their own leaders, who will protect their interests and maintain their independence.

68.right:
On January 25, there was a meeting in a large congregation in Tank, there were people present, the important Khans of the area were also included.

On December 12, I left again, and went to the area of Badragh, Ero, Rani Zane, Kot, Miana, Kot, and Spin Kharo, Alandi Valley, there is no doubt that the people are ready, and some workers have a pick-up shop, they give one in Badragh. In the middle of Shuni, Abd al-Latif Sahib Effendi and his like-minded people, an independent Islamic school is also running.

On the 16th of December, I met Mr. Sahib Ji of Span Kharo. His enlightened vision had a great and good effect on the country. During the three-day conversation, there was also a discussion about Afghanistan.

69.right:
Let it be known. That our esteemed Khan Murad is residing in America, a matter of great fortune. It is a blessing to learn of his charitable contributions each year, as he generously provides for the needs of our community. I said, therefore, that the path to our charity should not be diverted. We entrust our benefactions to the care of our leader, Abdul Ghaffar Khan, who manages them with wisdom and diligence, bringing considerable benefit to our family and nation.

Thus, many wise men concurred, agreeing that such charitable endeavors are vital for the prosperity of our nation and people. Indeed, as Khan Murad has rightly observed, a modest investment yields a substantial return, and he instructs us to convey this sentiment. Others echo this sentiment, urging us to follow suit in our own generosity, thereby furthering our collective well-being.

I offer myself to you in service, and I hold such hopes for the future. Should I ever be granted the opportunity to bestow a gift, you are most welcome to receive it. I am sending you a check for six and five rupees, should you find it fitting to accept. Let us strive to emulate the generosity of our American brethren, and proceed upon their path.

And now, as the time for the annual review approaches, I recall the words of our Pashtun brethren in America – that they dedicate considerable sums to the advancement of knowledge and the betterment of their community.  It is a matter of great importance that we, too, contribute to these noble causes.

Let it be recorded:

It is not without reason that we have been granted this opportunity, and it is a matter of great importance that we continue to support the advancement of knowledge, and that we do so with diligence and foresight.

71.right:
Afghanistan and Nadir Khan

United Kabul Noor Basr

“In the name of God, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful,” I say with all sincerity.

Let me express my heartfelt desire to stand with the people of my nation.

It is true that today, without money, it is impossible to achieve even basic necessities. Observe, when a people are impoverished, they cannot afford to fund new initiatives, hospitals, or schools, nor can they serve the interests of their nation, their land, or their faith. And if a people lack financial resources, they are reduced to mere shadows of their former selves.

Some may ask why Pashtuns do not emulate other peoples, claiming this is a falsehood. However, their generosity, though often delayed, is not a permanent solution; it reflects the traditions of a bygone era, hindering the progress of the nation.

Times have changed, and it is imperative that Muslims have adapted as well. They have failed to do so, because their generosity does not benefit the nation, but rather diminishes it. In the past, service to the faith was freely offered, and we would collect funds, deliver them, and continue the service to the nation and to the stipends of the faithful.

Today, everything is driven by money. There is no room for religious propagation, education, or even the provision of a decent burial, unless funded by money. No one can truly serve their nation and God without financial support.

Therefore, it is now incumbent upon Muslims to embrace the spirit of the times. Pashtuns should not, as other peoples do, establish a national fund, but rather support their own people and alleviate their needs, and Islam should continue to provide stipends, support, and guidance. I hope that our Pashtun brethren will emulate the example of the Pashtuns in America, following their lead.

When I received news of the charitable contributions of our American brethren through the “Association for the Propagation of Education” in the name of the “Society for the Welfare of Pashtuns,” I was deeply moved. Let the record show:

Receipt
(Map)
The Association for the Welfare of Pashtuns, established by the residents of America for the distribution of funds for the benefit of Pashtuns. Received, for the purpose of dissemination, by the Treasurer of the Pashtun Welfare Society of America.

It is not without money that one can serve God or the nation, and no one should die without dedicating their life to the service of God and their people. If one is a servant, as our Pashtuns are, they will simply exist, and if they are poor, then…

Therefore, it is now incumbent upon Muslims to embrace the spirit of the times. Pashtuns should not, as other peoples do, establish a national fund, but rather support their own people and alleviate their needs, and Islam should continue to provide stipends, support, and guidance. I hope that our Pashtun brethren will emulate the example of the Pashtuns in America, following their lead.

When I received news of the charitable contributions of our American brethren through the “Association for the Propagation of Education” in the name of the “Society for the Welfare of Pashtuns,” I was deeply moved. Let the record show:

The mind of the “Pashtoon”

I hear reports of the activities of the “Pashtoon” in Kabul. The journal “Pashtoon” deserves the pride of our nation. Its reports of progress and the advancement of our homeland and the solidarity of our Pashtun people will serve as a testament to its value.

However, I am struck by a sense of surprise and wonder. When I examine the name of the secretary of “Pashtoon” – Durr-e-Jan and Abdulqadir Khan – I find them listed. But I suspect – may God guide us – that this is not the same Abdulqadir Khan, the “Pasawar Doom,” “Letter Writer,” who is known for his correspondence.

I ponder – if this is indeed Abdulqadir Khan, then I ask the following questions on behalf of my friends: First, the questions I have raised – and second, the suspicion that these questions are not his. If this secretary is not Abdulqadir Khan, then these questions have no connection to him –

75.right:
January 1930

It is believed in England that these are political prisoners in India, and that the political figures within India are now aligned with the British government. The hope is that the matter will be resolved amicably and swiftly.

Round Table Conference

Furthermore, it is considered vital to hold a Round Table Conference – bringing together representatives from twelve nations to deliberate on important matters. We, the servants of the National Jirga, await your command. Please establish this conference and let it proceed. However, we desire that this conference be held without delay. Within the Jirga, the Indian representatives should be able to freely discuss their concerns.

Those individuals forbidden employment by the government, such as the Khan Sahibs, Khan Bahadur, and Nawabs – those who hold positions of service within the government – are well-known throughout the world. We do not concur with this practice. Today, the government grants honor to those who champion the freedom of India and bestow rights upon them. What do you propose we do in response? And if you wish to impose any laws upon us, we will adhere to our faith.
Pashtun 27

The government maintains relations with other nations, such as Australia and South Africa, and so on. We, the Pashtuns, are included in their diplomatic circles, and we will be formally recognized by the government on January 1st, 1930, in the first month of the year. We do not acknowledge the authority of foreign governments, and we value our complete freedom. Whenever the Hindus of India agree on a matter, and the nation is prepared to make sacrifices for it, then let it be a day when the government of national leaders is enlightened, and let it be a day when the leaders are released from prison and let it be a day when disputes are resolved. It is better this way, we are included in the rights of other countries, but you do not declare your own freedom. This is why we say: If the Indians agree and the nation is prepared for sacrifice, then let it be a day when the government of national leaders is enlightened, and let it be a day when the leaders are released from prison and let it be a day when disputes are resolved. It is better this way, we are included in the rights of other countries, but you do not declare your own freedom. Why is this?
He said: This is what the Indians agreed upon, and it is a nation prepared for sacrifice.

77.right:
Pashtun
Volume (2) February 1920, the Blessed Month of Ramadan

A Letter from Shah Sahib Gul

(1)

I state, in the service of Pir Sahib Gul, that it is customary to prepare for the acceptance of the Gul’s flowers, to circulate amongst the people, and to listen to the tale of Hori, and to offer a respectful homage to the Hori. The Pir, it is said, does not heed the plight of the impoverished, nor does he offer assistance or support, nor does he recognize any old man or long-haired individual as deserving of respect.

It is not an uncommon occurrence, though misfortunes do arise, sir. You may be surprised to learn that this was not due to lack of concern or honesty – it was not a matter of falsehoods or deceit. Therefore, O Pashtun people, what part did we play? We have endured much more than once.

The truth is that those in power do not wish to see our Pashtun identity – this sense of justice and righteousness, and injustice – flourish. They claim that certain actions should be judged according to the law, and that the people should place their trust in the authorities. They say that the people should look to those in power for guidance. However, look at the present state of the people – they discuss everything with each other, going to the Governor to seek his opinion. “Let us wait and see!” they cry – the times have changed, as you may well observe. Indeed, the rule of the mercenary rulers is prevalent in England today – that is, the power and authority of the empire rests in the hands of those who are employed. However, this matter dates back to a previous time, and it is decreed that the British Parliament will consider a number of proposals, which will grant certain rights to these mercenaries. You may not realize it, but the actions taken by these individuals are reminiscent of the actions taken by those who served the Crown – and now they serve the Empire.

Pashtun people, the world changes, and it has changed! It is not merely that we have been slow to understand this; we have been deliberately kept in the dark. Regarding the late Abbas Khan – and his inner circle, including the notables and landowners – a period of stagnation and emptiness has ended. It was necessary that Abbas Khan cease his involvement in the affairs of the notables and landowners, and return to his own pursuits, and to his family. Let him rest, and let him be free from these burdens.

After a month, we will travel to the Tehsil of Char Saday to serve the government and its officials, and all other employees will be dismissed except for myself, who will remain to deal with the circumstances.

Because I reflect on the state of affairs each evening, I wrote to my elder, who informed me of the situation. I expressed my gratitude for his concern, and I pondered whether the police officials might lodge a complaint with the Magistrate – perhaps a small plea for redress. It is said that this steadfast man, Abbas Khan, is a source of sustenance for many of his servants. His livelihood depends on our contributions. I have always striven to conduct myself with integrity and honesty, and I have never indulged in bribery. See, and you will find that I will not be deceived, and that my reputation will not be tarnished. And so, let them cease their pursuit of me, and let my road be free.

I expressed my gratitude for his concern, and I pondered whether the police officials might lodge a complaint with the Magistrate – perhaps a small plea for redress. It is said that this steadfast man, Abbas Khan, is a source of sustenance for many of his servants. His livelihood depends on our contributions. 78.right:
“Look here! We have identified twenty-seven individuals – from the district – and several of them committed grievous wrongs against the poor villagers. It was, after all, a matter of this: you are not well, sir – you must speak the truth, you will be astonished – if it does not work, you were unaware of the facts – so, O Pashtun people, what was our fault? We have twice as many.
The truth is that these outsiders do not desire this – that we Pashtuns also harbor this sentiment – that the government grants rights to the deserving and injustice to the undeserving. These outsiders say, ‘Whatever the decree, the people should place their trust in it.’ You will say, ‘What are the people discussing today? A servant goes to them and says, ‘Come, come!’ Times have changed – you will see – today the power and authority of the kingdom in England rests in the hands of the workers – that is, the authority of the empire is held by the laborers – but this is the account of a past event – that a group of people in the Great Assembly (Parliament) of England have proposed a series of measures, granting some rights to the laborers.  I do not ask you – what should be done with him, what he did yesterday in the name of the laborers and their rights.  Yesterday, the right to rule became theirs.
Pashtun people, the world changes, and it has changed – therefore, it is not just us who have experienced this; the daughter of Abbas Khan, and the interior of Abbas Khan, where we are entangled in rituals, hair, and wealth – the old order has been abolished – it was necessary that Abbas Khan should abandon the jinn and the wealth for the sake of Abbas Khan – let him run, and leave his people and his possessions – and if he does not, then he will perish.
After the month of Ra, we brought Tul to Charsdeh Tehsil – and the government is in disarray.
When it was evening, I inquired about the situation of the doctor – I asked, ‘No one has brought him from the village.’  Mr. C. – what did we do at that time?  What a day it has been! We also had the seventh arrangement – it was closed – I was anxious to know why it had been closed – either Mia Mala or my own servant came – and it was discovered that some police officers had permitted entry on foot – I am grateful for their assistance on such a day – I thought, ‘Perhaps the police officers would have appealed to Makkah Sahib’ – he is a persistent man – pay their salary late – I am growing weary – what more, my friend – but I worked well with them and removed all their documents – I answered, ‘I am not hungry for bribes – I will see – will you listen to me again?’ and I closed my mouth – I saw – the next day, Wali Sahib came. I asked them – what was the reason for closing it?  What was the answer?  That day, when Abbas Khan was released, the situation had arrived. ”

79.right:
Pashtun

February 1930

Lahore Congress Session

The Congress is a gathering of Hindus, Muslims, Sikhs, Parsis, and Christians of India. It convenes annually in a province of India, drawing together the people of the entire nation. This assembly’s purpose is to secure the freedom of our homeland (India) from foreign rule. These individuals tirelessly strive to achieve this goal.

The session of this Congress took place in Lahore. Representatives from America, Germany, and the province had arrived for observation. It is regrettable that this gathering was so proximate to our border; nonetheless, a relatively small number of Pashtuns participated.

I have alluded to certain matters within this session, and I intend to elaborate upon specific details concerning any pertinent issue. My entire manuscript will be filled to capacity, leaving no room for other subjects. I trust that you will find this account agreeable, as it reflects the sentiments expressed in the example of the Green Law. I earnestly desire to present these essential discussions to you in the spirit of service.

You will be aware of the considerable efforts being undertaken by this Congress. The British have granted us certain rights, though it is imperative that our government also secure a rightful share.

Concerning religion, there was no need for elaborate doctrines. Indeed, even in the pursuit of historical claims, men often boast of exceptional merit – a practice we have long disregarded.

Secondly, the Hindus, even in this era, show little interest in Sanskrit; they dismiss its study as unnecessary, a state that persists to this day – beyond the historical evidence, the names of villages, towns, and regions within Afghanistan are frequently derived from Sanskrit and the languages of the Indians, such as: local designations like Mat, Badanir, Bajaur, Mohmand, Dur, Nangarhar, Kandahar (the name of Kandahar), Panawar, Hattankar, Shakardara, Banun, Kohat – Arah, Charkani, Malakand, Karmi Korah, and Manki.

The vocabulary itself, and the names of livestock, are similarly rooted in Sanskrit.  We find terms like Panj (five), Chanbi (seven), Mongani (a type of sheep), Tarangi (a type of horse), Panra (a bundle), Chana (a measure of grain), Camel, V Lash (a type of rope), Trilash (three ropes), Khaxe (a whip), Whipa (a type of plough), Four, Five, Six, All, Chalo (a measure), Mishre (a type of grain), and Kashar – all reflecting a strong Sanskrit influence.  Calculating the number of such words – and considering the remaining Arabic terms – reveals a significant portion of our language’s heritage.

Regarding tribal names, we find Kakar, Dur, Oud Khel, Mangal, Mohmand, and Bajauri, among others.

I believe that now, the matter is becoming considerably clearer. We are not merely the descendants of a single, distinguished family – rather, we are the descendants of all peoples. We are not of the Bani Israel.

February 1920, and not the descendants of Hazrat Khalid bin Waleed, may God be pleased with him – particularly a man such as Hazrat Khalid, may God be pleased with him. How can three hundred thousand (approximately thirty million) children be born over thirteen hundred years? I estimate thirty million, as the inhabitants will undoubtedly be the descendants of Pashto in Pashto.

It is imperative to maintain contact with Sanskrit, and to learn only the essential terms.  Contacting Sanskrit does not imply a rejection of Arabic; the purpose is simply that the complexities of Arabic derivation cannot be fully expressed through its grammatical structure.  Our word formation, for instance, is fundamentally similar to that of Sanskrit, mirroring the process of derivation from the root word – a relationship that cannot be perfectly replicated through Arabic’s system.  This similarity persists, as does the reliance on Sanskrit for many fundamental concepts.  Indeed, this has been the case for centuries.  It is crucial to understand this connection.

It does not appear to be the case, however, this account is inaccurate. While sustenance was provided, it was far from adequate. 

What befell him was a grievous misfortune; the prisoners suffered greatly, and the inmates of the jail languished in perpetual misery.  Each day, three men perished, their spirits crushed by hardship.  The love of family, sadly, was suspended.  Today, this sorrow resides within the confines of the jail – a situation that has persisted for far too long.

The prison’s impoverished inmates endured a wretched existence, their plight compounded by the constant threat of illness. One day, I found myself confined to the kitchen, the guest of the jailer, whose health was failing. He displayed a remarkable composure, revealing the lavish fare of spiced meats, succulent vegetables, and rich sauces. Yet, even this abundance was meager, and the deceased’s condition was readily apparent. Was this truly the sustenance of a human being?

In response, the gentleman offered a considered opinion: this food was, in fact, exceptionally beneficial for health.  However, it was not to be consumed by the afflicted. Perhaps he had surmised that such a rich diet would be unsuitable. I then relayed my own concerns – that the inmates of the jail were sick and suffering, and that this very food was contributing to their misery.

It is regrettable that no heed is paid to the words of the impoverished prisoners.  Though efforts are made to provide them with work, they receive no more than a common laborer.  These unfortunate souls are neglected and forgotten.  Let us not turn a blind eye to their plight.

Furthermore, many of the landholders, driven to desperation, resorted to concealing their harvests in the fields and ditches – yet they receive no compensation for the damage inflicted upon their land.  These impoverished landowners are left to bear the burden alone.

It is a lamentable state of affairs for these impoverished landowners – reduced to destitution, their spirits broken.  They are deprived of comfort and sustenance, and many are forced to endure a life of hardship.

This is the condition of the impoverished landowners – subjected to hardship, deprived of dignity, and abandoned by those in power.  The officials and servants of the government, oblivious to their suffering, continue to live in luxury.  They demand tribute, and the people are forced to comply.  It is as if the government itself is a burden upon the populace, a constant drain on their resources.

— Pashtun – March 1930

It is worth noting that Mr. Gandhi, the great leader of India, is currently traveling through Japan. His heart, now aged, is still filled with youthful vigor. He has written numerous books, and is considered one of the foremost figures in the world. He has also been awarded the Nobel Prize – a remarkable achievement.  Yet, despite his fame, his heart remains gentle and compassionate.  He has not been seen in these parts before.

For many years, he has been residing in the city of Ahmedabad and the town of Sidh, in the province of Gujarat.  During a visit to these locations, he met with Mr. Gandhi at the Ashrama (a monastic school) of the late Mahatma.  Amidst the complexities of the present situation – when the issue of freedom for the country is paramount – it is a curious circumstance.  So many gatherings and national meetings are taking place, yet the people remain impoverished.  The Pashtun, in the confines of his hermitage, spends his nights in study and contemplation, or perhaps in earning a meager livelihood.  He does all that he does for the sake of his family and his own well-being.  Mr. Gandhi, too, spends his evenings in the darkness, seeking solace in the company of his fellow countrymen.  He often visits the homes of the poor and the destitute, offering them comfort and support.  It is a noble and selfless act.

I wish that the same spirit of generosity and compassion would prevail among all.  Mr. Gandhi, the leader of the Ashrama, the champion of civilization, and the advocate of justice, is a truly remarkable man.
