There was sincerity and effort, and the ongoing Jirga continued, with many divine servants participating and contributing to its sessions and deliberations, culminating in several Jirgas established on the 31st of January, including sessions held on the 1st of February in the presence of Shah Mansur, and further sessions held on the 2nd of February, with the Jirgas convened and divine servants mobilized. On the 3rd of February, they returned.

The grace and favor of God were exceedingly great during this period, surpassing those of previous eras, due to the establishment of a Jirga in every town and the mobilization of God’s servants – they were patrons, beloved by the rulers, with an abundance of affection and love among the poor, without any desire for excess, and we shared their grief, sending representatives from the provincial Jirga to offer support. I feel no effect from it, though it may touch your heart… Indeed, my heart remains cold; I perceive a certain sadness in your truly expressed words. What, then, does this rum-based opposition stir within you? And your lips – they seem troubled. I might even venture to say it resembles the mouth of Isa – no exaggeration, I assure you. A letter from a friend of Khama
A letter from Mohammad Nader Khan Padshah

To my dear sister-in-law and former friend, Charanki Delia Sheta, peace be upon you!

I eagerly await the arrival of your Pashtun magazine.
My friend, Mohammad Nader Khan Padshah, has arrived in Afghanistan and is now an orphan.
I was your old friend, and I remain with you in spirit.
I am writing to you from Jalalabad, Etihad East, with a history to share.
In the Pashtun press, your magazine shines brightly.
His Highness Nadir Shah is also keenly interested in your publication, and he frequently requests to receive a copy.
I am pleased to fulfill this request. The sword is brought before them, and they read with affection for their father.
Previous subjects appreciate your efforts.

Grandma, I am sending you a gift for Zeri – a young woman with dark eyes.
She eagerly awaits your every service and assistance.
May God continue and direct this message.
I aspire to be a Pashto person who nourishes the heart and not the stomach.
I am ready to serve you.
Your friend, Burhanuddin Munshi
His Highness

Syed Jamaluddin Sahib possessed this disposition and came to know himself. He intended to travel to Egypt to observe and derive benefit, with the aim of studying books and seeking tranquility, and had no intention of remaining for long. However, upon meeting the King of Riyadh in Egypt, he found the King to be exceedingly pleased and ordered the payment of ten thousand *tanah* (currency) to the Egyptian government. This sum was of no use whatsoever in Afghanistan; such a salary should not be imposed.

After a period of time, more and more people returned to Bannu from the Taliban, and they benefited from his teachings. From them, they began to teach higher sciences, rhetoric, wisdom, theory, natural philosophy, mysticism, and the principles of jurisprudence. They established schools in their own homes and were surrounded by their people. Those who possessed greater knowledge and understanding than the Taliban, and were open to free thought, criticism, and debate, were also discouraged.

The Egyptian writer, Ishaq Noah (correspondent), in his book *al-Darr* published by the writer, wrote concerning Syed Sahib’s reputation in the newspapers of that time: “In those days, many articles were published in the newspapers of Egypt, awakening the thoughts of the common people.”

Today, January 31st, the day of May, the tax day was not observed.
The residents held a meeting in the secretariat of the Caliphate Committee, presided over by Mr. Malik Sher Zaman Khan Sahib.

—

The meeting was lively and full of discussion,
With residents gathered in one or two places in Nal – but no Pashto was heard,
As if the attendees were secluded in their homes.  It was a grand and important meeting, and the complaints about the rally were evident.
Maulavi Habibullah Saheb, the leader of the meeting, presented Mr. Malik Sher Zaman Khan as the chairman. Khan was unanimously approved, and the meeting commenced.
Mr. President: Addressing the audience, he declared that the Pashtun people must unite, overcome discord, and be free from ignorance.
He spoke of the Khilafat Committee, the Congress, and the declaration of independence on December 31st, and then discussed the past and present conditions of the British, their salt-headedness and the prohibition of salt.
He explained the reasons for protesting against these injustices.

—

Sir Hilton Grant, Chief Commissioner of the Mobah Frontier, had sent a representative, Mr. Hanna Pule, to study Pashto in London.
He informed the audience of the British people’s disregard for the people of Hindustan, their failure to uphold the law, and the imposition of punitive fines on the residents.
He presented the reasons for protesting against these injustices.

120.right:
Pashtun
Volume 2
June 1939
Pacha Khan's speech
Sweet and heavy! You know what is happening in our country because of the British colonial system. They have seized our wealth and our people are living in poverty and misery. The request of my heart is that we should unite and fight against this oppression. They are trying to divide us by any means. But we must understand that our interest is in agreement. If we do not unite, they will still play with our fate. I am calling you today to join hands for the freedom of our country. We have made a lot of sacrifices in the past years, but these sacrifices are worth it when we reach our goal. For this we must believe in ourselves and stand up for our rights. This struggle is not easy, but if we remain firm on the path of truth, then success is certain.

What actions have been taken by the British government in Pakhtunkhwa, they are to destroy the rights of the people. We should protect ourselves from such conspiracies and join hands for our national interests.

123.right:
Pashtun Amateur 1929

It seems hearts yearn for reunion, and now they will gather in earnest.
We shall set aside our pride – and not indulge in Pashtun rivalry – dismissing idle speculation and unfounded rumors as mere fancy. The service of our Pashtun brethren is a source of such profound contentment, that no other joy can compare. Indeed, who else has achieved such a noble and fulfilling endeavor? And what is the Pashtun people to do, under the leadership of Mr. Kharafistan, whose judgment is so often questioned?

The owner, the merchant, the wealthy financier – in this modern world, Islam is often overshadowed by the pursuits of materialism. The laborer, the dependent, and the impoverished find themselves increasingly entangled in such affairs. The meetings of farmers and laborers are often brief, and fraught with difficulty.  Many people are impoverished by the waters, and oppressed by the chiefs and other powerful figures, until they are reduced to destitution and exile.  This is a lamentable state of affairs, a stain upon the honor of the Pashtun people, who are said to be free from the shackles of Satan. Is this truly the case? Such contradictions abound.  There are always those who seek to exploit the goodwill of others, to profit from the misfortunes of the many.

Pashtun Amateur 1929

Let it be remembered that the earth is finite, and the people desire the unity of their nation.  They pursue this goal with unwavering determination, for to associate with such treachery is to invite ruin.  Let no one presume to share in the wealth of another, for that is a path to disgrace.  The wealthy should be mindful of their position, and the poor should strive to improve their lot.  The esteemed Khan Khitab, a resident of the Chah Dera, has been under house arrest for years.  The fate of the esteemed Jamal, a resident of the Chah Dera, is also uncertain.  How many have been driven from their homes, and forced to wander the land in search of sustenance?  Their grief is profound.  They have no shelter, no protection, and no hope.  The people of the land lament their plight.  Their service is always for the good of the nation.  It is known that Khan, the wise and just, has always sought the welfare of his people.  But now, the enemy rejoices.  He revels in the misfortunes of others.  May God guide him to repentance, and grant him wisdom.  Let the people be mindful of their duty, and not be deceived by appearances.  The world is fleeting.  In it, there are those who seek to wage war with words, rather than with arms.  Let us not forget the lessons of the past.  Let us not be swayed by vanity.  Let us remember that the Pashtun people are a nation of warriors, and that their strength lies in unity and courage.  Let us cherish our heritage, and protect it from corruption.  Let us not be divided by petty disputes.  Let us be generous to our neighbors, and compassionate to the poor.  Let us always strive to uphold the honor of our nation.

127.right:
Regarding the advancement of civilization and education, a critical necessity for the Pashtun people and their own well-being is the establishment of a nationwide system of education – one that provides a comprehensive and standardized learning experience for all citizens. This represents a path whereby every individual within the nation can secure their rightful entitlements. To underscore the significance of education, concerted efforts must be directed toward the creation of a curriculum designed to cultivate the intellectual capacities of the populace. This endeavor should be driven by a commitment to national progress, not merely the interests of a select segment of society. The existing system, as it stands, prioritizes the concerns of a particular class, thereby excluding a significant portion of our community from the benefits of education. We must strive to align education with the needs of the people, ensuring that every citizen can reap the rewards of learning within their own nation. Despite any obstacles encountered, we must persevere toward a prosperous future, harnessing the power of knowledge to propel the advancement of our country. Our objective should extend beyond the mere construction of schools; rather, we must foster an environment where every individual possesses equitable opportunities for learning. Through this, every member of our society will contribute to the nation’s development, forging a stronger and more resilient community.

A concise note on the paramount importance of fostering true civilization and education:

Fundamentally, national education serves as a vital step towards the collective good and the enhancement of society. Regrettably, the current system of education falls short in fulfilling its potential, leaving the people of the nation deprived of their inherent rights. Consequently, a transformative shift is required within the realm of education – one that unlocks the doors of knowledge for every citizen. The prevailing educational framework caters to a limited group, whereas education, as a fundamental social right, must be accessible to all. To facilitate the expansion of education, the government must enact policies that consider the needs of the populace and implement concrete measures to advance the nation’s progress. In this manner, the people of our society will not only be illuminated by the light of knowledge but will also flourish on the global stage as a robust and progressive community. It is the responsibility of every Pashtun to champion the dissemination of education and advocate for this crucial undertaking.

133.right:
Pashtun; Tammany 26 Allergen 1929

Mirwais brought forth Elpo – Mirwais, accompanied by a richly adorned litter, laden with gifts and bribes, to present a decree from his office to Gurgen Khan.  Initially, Gurgen had granted time for the interests and needs of the people, a pledge we had consistently made to Mirwais.  Let us observe God’s work, and fulfill our agreement, safeguarding our honor and reputation. However, upon learning that the first prisoner, a man of considerable consequence, had been dispatched to Isfahan, and subsequently returned from Persia, a shadow of doubt fell upon Mirwais’s mind.  He became convinced that Gurgen Khan’s power was waning, and that the Gurgen Khanate was in peril.  Russia, recognizing that the Afghan Sultan could not be easily subdued, sought alliances with the Persian Empire and devised schemes for negotiation.  Gurgen, aware of his own vulnerability, would not be subjected to humiliation, nor would he endure further losses of men and resources.  Yet, this was due to the fact that a small contingent had been dispatched to secure the fortress of Charbagh, and one of Gurgen Khan’s loyal men was stationed within the formidable walls of the Russian outpost in Peshwar.  Furthermore, a family of Christian descent held ties to a non-Christian family, and this was the root of the matter.
133.left:
Pashtun; Tammany 27 Allergen 1929

A Weary Reflection

It was conceived that Mirwais had amassed considerable wealth and riches in preparation for a conflict with the Gurgen Khanate. He presented a lavish display of treasures and bribes to Gurgen Khan, demonstrating the authority of his office.  Gurgen, recognizing the needs and desires of his people, had previously pledged his support to Mirwais.  Let us contemplate God’s deeds – let us fulfill our agreement and preserve our honor and dignity.  However, when the esteemed First Governor was dispatched to Isfahan, in the course of attending to the affairs of our own people, we realized that we must consider the concerns of the powerful Gurgen Khan, and his harsh restrictions.  Remembering the time when the burden of the treasury was sent back to Iran, it was a loss of a lifetime.  Therefore, we must refrain from rebellion.  Yet, considering the plight of a single, yet influential, individual, and the potential consequences for the stability of the region, we must carefully weigh our options.  Mirwais’s birthday celebrations were a spectacle of great renown throughout the region, a symbol of life and struggle for the Pashtun people, akin to the legendary Lani Khana and Gurun.

Fakriye

The opinion of the Ana Dar region is that if these strong Afghans were to travel to the distant lands of Qalanderi, they would print this text on the paper of the new year, and it would be read with great interest.  It would be a valuable resource for the learned and the wise.  Let us not forget the teachings of the great Sufi scholar, Khushal Khan Khattak.  He was a master of eloquence and a renowned poet.  He traveled extensively, witnessing the hardships of the world and gaining experience.  He is skilled in managing affairs and a teacher of ethics.  The only difference is that he has spent much of his life in Iran, and Khushal Khan Khattak has been a prominent figure in the mountains of the Pashtun people.

It was midnight and it became the night of tigers. God made me wish that someone would come and take care of me. It was God's work that at three o'clock in the night something like a light appeared in the room from the passage. I wondered who it was. In a very short time, three people came. One of them said that he definitely knows me. I told him that I do not know you. When I said that, he specifically said that I am a friend of someone you know. At this time, I breathed a sigh of relief and was hoping to see what would happen. They didn't ask me what crime are you here for? I told him that I am innocent. They said that you are imprisoned here for a political crime, but the fact is that you are imprisoned here for the death penalty. When I heard this, such sadness came over me that I wished to die. They said that we have received an order to release us from someone who knows you. I said how can I be free? They said you should go with us. I went with them. We encountered many problems on the way, but at last we reached a place where behind a big door we were unblocked and we secretly made our way.
139.left:
Pashtun Atanzi 39 

Before this was the case in Peshawar, there were rains that filled everywhere with water, and orders came at night that any houses in the southern part of Peshawar should be evacuated at this time. People were very afraid because of the increase of this water. At this time, we went there to help them to save their lives. At night there were people's cries, and when we came to their aid, the condition of the people was indescribable. Children screaming, women crying, and men raising their hands for help, it was a heart-rending sight. We tried to find a safe place for them. When we reached a place, there was an old school in Peshawar that we used as a shelter for the refugees. Crowds of people took refuge in this place. We made arrangements for them to eat and drink, and thus saved them from the threat of death. It was an experience that will never be forgotten. The joy on their faces, seeing our help, gave us a great sense of satisfaction.

Let it be known that in Islam, a ruler of the faithful is forbidden from fomenting rebellion against another Muslim nation, nor should such actions be undertaken. Let all contemplate this matter. Furthermore, two kings, engaged in opposition to Bijan Khan, were compelled to seek redress from the esteemed scholars of the realm, requesting a fatwa regarding the matter of Meri Sharif and the transgressions of treason. This fatwa was duly observed, and its dictates were not disregarded. Now, the path of Bijan Khan was laid bare.  The grievances of the loyal Muslims, whenever they arose, were addressed with diligence, and reports of injustice spread throughout the land, reaching the gates of cities. Instead of challenging the rule of the free Khan, the people should turn their attention to opposing his government. Moreover, the King of Lara to Kandahar sought permission and a summons to undertake the pilgrimage to Mecca, and the leaders of his tribe pledged their allegiance and swore fealty to the Sharif, returning from his journey.  His word and declaration were not to be questioned, and this permission was granted.  These individuals then sought the endorsement of the city’s leading scholars, producing a fatwa to legitimize their actions.  Who would compose verses and odes in praise of a cruel king and the Sunnis of the Bait? The territory bordering Azad and Ghastan is subject to closure of trade and economic activity. The common people and their livelihoods are strained, and the value of human life is diminished, with the law rendered ineffective. This misuse of weapons stems from ignorance and a lack of understanding of legal principles. A Pashtun is no longer loyal to his homeland, but merely to the weapon in his hand. Yet, within Pashtun society, the use of weapons is considered a matter of 18th-century custom. The consequences of this ignorance are readily apparent, leading to increased bloodshed and a cycle of violence. Pashtuns abandon the protection of their families and homes, leaving them vulnerable.

Secondly, I present the national traditions of the Pashtun people. The customs of the people – their status and the status of those considered elders – are overwhelmingly centered around the practice of murder and the complicity of those who carry out such acts. It is considered honorable for a Pashtun to act against another, to seek revenge, and to abandon his homeland. Honor and loyalty demand retribution for any perceived slight, fueling cycles of violence and bloodshed. The Pashtun people are not bound to the state; they are free to roam and trade, and to display their youth and honor through personal acts of bravery. The pride of the Pashtun is demonstrated through such deeds, reflecting their values and customs.

Economic prosperity is a distant dream. Proverbs abound, lamenting the loss of life and the degradation of the nation. The standard of living for many is impoverished, shaped by misguided Western influences and a lack of proper education. The people become isolated and disconnected, drifting aimlessly and losing their way. The division of the state leads to a decline in morality and a disregard for proper conduct. The Pashtun people are forced to adopt foreign customs, abandoning their own traditions and values. They become slaves to the whims of the powerful, losing their dignity and independence. The pursuit of wealth and status leads to corruption and exploitation, while the spirit of the Pashtun is diminished.

The land endures change; it is yours, and yours alone. These mountains, the green heart of Kohistan, are the rightful inheritance of every Pashtun. This is the Pashtun National Number – a testament to the bravery and honor of the Pashtun people. Each Pashtun is a son of the brave region, and a beloved guest in every home.  And this is due to the honor and protection of Khan Khanan, the esteemed leader of all.  It is a matter of Pashtun geography, homeland, politics, and national identity – a reflection of the life of the Pashtun people, vibrant and resolute.  Khushal Khan Khattak, throughout his life, has striven for the Pashtun people, seeking to quell the discord of internal conflicts, and to restore the honor and grace of the Pashtun nation – to forge the Pashtun into a strong and resilient people, freeing them from the shackles of Mughal servitude!  Let it be known that his efforts, though ultimately unsuccessful, were nonetheless celebrated.  Indeed, the sun of Hindustan and Asia shines brightly over the peaks of the mountains, a beacon of Pashtun fortune and the glory of their rule.  Today, the Pashtun people look to the heavens, witnessing the prosperity and dominion of Pashtun.

158.right:
Khushal Khan’s poetry and patriotism, and his character:

Patriotism is poetry, and poetry is patriotism. This sentiment—the intertwining of Afghan patriotism with a passionate yearning—has grown so profound that every flower seems to echo the fear and longing for the homeland. It is at this point that the discussion of Khushal Khan’s poetry, at the appropriate moment, will be fruitful.

Khushal Jangi’s verses – readily usable, every Pashtun heart will be touched by them. They reflect upon the past and present of the Pashtuns, gazing upon the top of the head. The letters of these poets reveal the hope of Pashtun war, and the judgments of those who contemplate whether or not these wars are justified. The ideas of these poets represent the merits and virtues of the Pashtun people. They are the true mirror reflecting the poetry of the Pashtun homeland, for the Pashtun possesses a simple, sharp, and warlike nature, combined with a civil status. In his youth, he endured the hardships of the Pashtun people, living in the homes and villages of the region, witnessing the power of the country and the family, and the vastness of the plains. He observed the struggles of the Pashtuns against the Mughals, the destruction of their kingdom, and the scent of their people. He sought wisdom, consulted with wise men, and studied the ethics and moral principles. He also explored the beauty of music and poetic expressions of nature, and the love and longing for affection.  As a poet, he documented his experiences, his travels, and the famous deeds of his time. He sought to promote Pashtun culture and to liberate the Pashtun people from Mughal slavery.  He yearned for his homeland in every journey, remembering his family and complaining about the toll of the Ram Khan, lamenting the cycle of time, and expressing his sorrow at the plight of the people.

Because Khushal Khan is the interpreter of every feeling and a witness to the past, observe him closely. Khushal Khan is a joyous figure at the time of the Maidan Ghali, a symbol of perfect bravery and martial prowess, unified and resolute in every endeavor. Pashtun Almanzi
21 June 1939
Volume (5) Number (14)
A Message of Grief from the Pashtun People
The sacrifice and grief of the nation are such that the great leader, Khan Sahib, God’s Helper, and a champion of homeland freedom, who had always raised his voice against the injustices and oppression of the British, is no longer among us. His passing is a great sorrow for the entire nation. In this grief, not only his family and friends, but all those who have witnessed the aspirations of the homeland and its desire for independence and prosperity are in mourning. It is incumbent upon every Pashtun to remember his service and follow his path. We pray for his forgiveness in the presence of God. May God grant patience and blessings to his family and all his friends. Amen.

166.right:
Pashtun Atmanzi
21 June 1939
A Pashtun's message of grief on the death of Khan Sahib God Dad Khan
It is a pity that a great leader like Khan Sahib God Dad Khan, who understood the pain and sorrow of the nation's heart and a mujahid for the freedom of the country, who always raised his voice against the oppression and tyranny of the British, is not among us today. His death is a great tragedy for the entire nation. In this grief, not only his family and friends, but all those who dreamed of the independence and prosperity of the country, are in fear and mourning. God Dad Khan Sahib's life and struggle history is a clear example for those who are ready to sacrifice their lives in the path of truth. With his death, a page of the struggle of Pakhtunkhwa was turned. May God have mercy on him and may heaven be his place. Our condolences to his family and the rest of the family.
166.left:
Pashtun Atmanzi
21 June 1939
Volume (5) Number (14)
Pashtun's sad message
Khan Sahib God Dad Khan's struggle to serve the nation is an example that will always be a torch for future generations. He spent most of his life for the unity of Pashtuns and winning their rights. In his death, not only his family but the entire Pashtun nation is in grief. Remembering his services and following his path is the duty of every Pashtun. We pray for God's forgiveness for him. May Allah grant patience to his family and all his friends. Amen

God’s actions occurred during a time of intense hostility, when the Russian forces were formidable enemies. The British, in their folly, believed that the Russians would remain subservient, a notion they propagated to conceal their own vulnerabilities.  They considered the سرد (Sard – a term referring to a leader or chieftain) a crucial element in this deception, falsely claiming that no cruel individuals resided near them in the world.  However, within a short period, the English recognized their Russian adversary.  The astute and discerning would observe the state of those in power. It was widely known that the Lashkare-Hind (Indian Army) possessed sufficient strength to dominate the entire Indian subcontinent.  It was deemed that if they were to advance, they would establish a firm foothold there.  Indeed, the Pashtuns were increasingly recognized amongst the Indian populace.  When faced with doubt within their own territory, they were subjected to oppression by Russia.  It is well-documented that the Russians sought to purchase Nad Kkha (land) from the Pashtuns in their province, inflicting a grievous wound.  This resulted in the Pashtuns being left impoverished and without assistance. The government’s actions were perceived as deceitful, offering false accusations to mask their own transgressions.  All of this led to the Pashtuns suffering hardship and loss, and a profound sense of humiliation.  Today, the remnants of this suffering are evident.

169.right:
Pashtun Atmazi, 21st June 1920
(1)
Mir Ashraf Khan Sahib, a distinguished figure of the Bakhtiari tribe, has served with distinction throughout Pashtun lands and in many corners of the world. He has participated in numerous governmental roles, and his work is widely documented in the Pashtun newspaper, appearing in detail across various locales. The Pashtun community holds him in high esteem, frequently citing his service and achievements in their own publications. He has also contributed significantly to scholarly works, offering a comprehensive account of the Pashtun people.  Throughout his career, he has consistently championed the interests of the Pashtun nation, and his dedication has been unwavering, even to the point of sacrificing his own life for its advancement.  He remains a pivotal figure for the Pashtun community, continuing to advocate for their welfare and prosperity.
169.left:
Begins with this Salam Zubair, proposing reforms for the betterment of Pashtun affairs.  Pashtuns, we trust that (face) Sahibzada Abdul Qayyum Khan Sahib Qureshi, residing near Peshawar and the other colonies, will find a suitable home within this region and contribute to its intellectual and cultural life.  He is a man of learning and piety, and his service to the Pashtun community is commendable.  In his depiction of the Pashtun people within the esteemed “Shaan Hind” annual publication, he has presented a particularly insightful portrait.  It is hoped that he will continue to contribute to this work with further writings in the future.

---

Fazl Shah Sahib (Po Poon)’s poignant verses, inspired by the sight of the people, express a deep appreciation for the strength of Pashtunistan and the spirit of the nation.  He finds joy in the recognition of their collective achievements and dedicates his life to their well-being, openly acknowledging their shared destiny.  He is considered a leader within the Pashtun community, consistently guiding them towards progress and unity.  May he continue to be a steadfast presence in their lives, always striving for the betterment of the Pashtun people.  Let us hope that his efforts will ultimately lead to success for the Pashtun nation.

---

However, this proposal may cause harm to some, particularly those who rely on existing trade routes. Hakim Sahib anticipates a period of waiting, observing the developments in the daily news, and awaiting a response from the relevant authorities.  As reported by Ataullah Sahib, the grievances of the Iranian community are numerous. The noteworthy effort undertaken in the publication of this newspaper is to present the Pashtun people in the form of a national chronicle. This demonstrates that the Pashtun is not merely a servant of his people, but also a protector of their interests. This publication, akin to the Afghan truth, has been running for many years, including the recent introduction of new editions by Pashtun printers.

9

Brief News from Banner Members

Egypt’s *Al-Ahram* reports that Minister Abd al-Fattah Bani Pasha, the minister between Egypt and the Ottoman Empire, has passed away. News of this loan is circulating in Egypt, though it is said that due to the high mortality rate among the Egyptian populace, Pasha has postponed any salary increases.

Treaties of Turkey and Germany

News from Istanbul indicates that the German Parliament has confirmed the Indian agreement and that German refugees are being welcomed into the Pashtun territories. Specifically, the Pashtun community has received a loan of forty million pounds (thirty million from Booker) to be distributed by December 2nd, 1920. This Turkish investment will be channeled into the local economy during the winter months.

Bulgarian Circumstances

*Al-Ahram* reports that Bulgaria is experiencing severe flooding and devastation due to heavy rains, resulting in widespread displacement and destruction.

News from Russia and France and the Question of Concord

Discussions continue between Russia and France regarding a possible treaty, though it is reported that Russia’s conditions are less favorable than those of France. The Russian government is attempting to improve its position and is engaging in discussions with French representatives. It is anticipated that the terms of this agreement will be outlined in the coming months, with payments likely to be made in installments during the winter months.

German Occupation of British Territories

Berlin reports that in the areas of Germany now under British occupation, a new government is being established. The German populace is preparing to support this new government, which is currently engaged in negotiations with the British and French governments regarding peace terms.

Reports from British India

Numerous reports have been circulating regarding the situation in British India, particularly concerning the elections for the Congress party in Bengal.  The advice of Congress leaders is that Pashtun elders and Congress ministers should engage in discussions with their counterparts in India to address widespread corruption and injustices within the region, particularly among the Hindu population.

United Nationality and Awan

Arbab Abdul Ghafoor Khan is a strong and respected figure. He lives a clean and honorable life,
adhering to the principles of non-violence and opposing the unrest in India.
He does not utter words that would be considered offensive, nor does he seek to provoke discord.
While he has witnessed many individuals rise to prominence, such as Ferika,
the foreign powers recognize that only Abdul Ghafoor Khan possesses the true ability to effect change.
They understand that genuine progress can only be achieved through his efforts.
Therefore, they consider him an enemy of the tribes, particularly those seeking to establish a foreign government in India.
Abdul Ghafoor Khan, on the other hand, is a figure of unwavering integrity and dedication,
working tirelessly and honestly for the betterment of the nation.
If the foreign powers intend to impose their rule upon India and seek allies,
then it is undeniable that the education and foresight of Abdul Ghafoor Khan are invaluable.
Let those versed in the fundamental principles of Islam, and not swayed by the opinions of the ignorant,
consider his counsel with careful deliberation.

Note: The Republic, a concept now widely understood, was not considered essential in the days before it, even among those who advocated for new national identities and rejected the notion of caste.  Though the memory of ancestral lineage was not dismissed, it was not viewed as a foundational principle within Islam. The University of India, a socialist institution under the leadership of Jawaharlal Nehru, is a significant development in Indian history, chronicled in his autobiography as: “I believe that in the Muslims of India, there is a desire for the people of India to be in the new India, and she wishes that the inclination of the people of India towards socialism is desirable.”  Furthermore, resources were increasingly allocated to education and social welfare, particularly for the youth.  - (More to follow)
(Translation by Sayyid Rasool Sahib of the Islamic Text)

14 July 1938

The Congress convened with the aim of fostering unity among the people of the frontier region, seeking to strengthen their collective identity and resolve.  With gratitude to God, preparations for this momentous occasion have begun diligently, with the construction of tents, the provision of clothing, and the suppression of disorder.  The preliminary meetings of the Political Congress, held on the first day of July, brought together prominent figures such as Abdul Ghaffar Khan, Mian جعفر شاه صاحب, and other progressive leaders from across the Pashtun region, culminating in a grand gathering on July 6th.  A pledge of unity was made by the Pashtun people, signifying a commitment to a shared future.

The Memorial Service for Jan Khan

A memorial service was held in the province of سرحد and the surrounding areas to commemorate the passing of Jan Khan.  The Minister of Anzils and Khanan Sahibs organized this event for religious observance and to offer solace to the community.  While the bereaved families expressed their gratitude, they felt compelled to share this news.

India and the Frontier Regions

Efforts to improve the administration of the Indian frontier regions have yielded limited results, as there is little prospect of achieving true independence for the people of the region.  To combat the persistence of slavery and the practice of forced marriage, it is essential to address the underlying causes of these issues.  Therefore, it is necessary to provide young men with opportunities for education and employment, to discourage the continuation of slavery and forced marriage, and to advance the cause of freedom through these means.  Let us strive towards this goal, providing the young generation with the opportunity to inherit the legacy of their ancestors, to claim their own destiny, and to build a future free from oppression.

Furthermore, the end of Socialism should be decisively abolished. The Russians were shrewd, perceptive, and adept at deception. Rather than fostering their own internal development, they focused on strengthening their military power. This conflict pitted the formidable strength of Russia against the forces of the world. Lessons in strategy were learned, and the situation evolved with each passing year. The kingdom was brought under the rule of the divine, with roads and infrastructure constructed, and education expanded throughout the land, fostering prosperity and happiness for the people. Observing the past century of relations with India, one finds that the name of the Khan, a figure of considerable influence, was intertwined with the interactions between the Afghan tribes and the Russians, often marked by a lack of genuine trust and mutual understanding. The Russians settled among the tribes, observing from the gates of Moscow, often with a critical eye, seeking to understand their movements and anticipating their actions, striving to anticipate their every step and to assist them in their journeys. They were regarded as scribes, chronicling the events, as if searching for the lost descendants of the Bactrian and Sogdian peoples. Their efforts were directed towards influencing the British, hoping to sway them from their support of the proud Russians.

190.right:
The Pashto language

Six months past, the Pashto language was proposed in secret and circulated amongst Pakhtunkhwa during a conference. Though a plan for its reform lay in my mind, and Mirza Ghulam-ur-Rahim Zawal Sahib also shared this thought, urging me to present my own proposals, reflecting the conference’s aims. Now, the pressing need of the hour is for thoughtful men of letters to contribute. In this respect, the raw, unrefined intellects of our colleagues offer valuable assistance.

(1) The script should be standardized; the old, traditional form, a testament to Pashto’s historical roots. Most people find it easier to read, resembling the familiar Persian script. Pashto’s homeland lies between Iran and India, and its alphabet reflects this geographical position, with a decimal point of change.

(2) Pashto words do not typically form compound words with vowels; they lack a distinct “o,” “u,” or “e” form. Like “d,” “z,” “r,” the Pashto alphabet utilizes a variety of symbols, mirroring those found in Persian and Urdu. Special cases exist, however…

July 29, 1939

For a phrase such as this, it should be written in its original form, preventing later alterations. Words should be carefully considered, weighing their breadth, depth, and force.  It is necessary to choose the appropriate term, rather than simply substituting one word for another, as this distorts the unique characteristics of Pashto. The language itself is a closed system, relying on two Arabic roots for its fundamental elements – strength, depth, and force, alongside the specific features of the Arabic language.

(2) Certain Pashto words are used differently by various tribes, and other tribes attempt to refine their usage. For example, “Kinasteh” is used in one tribe, while in another, it is “Bai Kineshte.”  Words are sometimes selected that are closely related to Persian, such as “Mela.” “Wold” signifies a Pashtun’s loyalty to the Persian language.

(5)  It is best to avoid unnecessary embellishments in writing, such as the superfluous “I” at the beginning of a sentence.
(6) In any word where a stressed vowel occurs, it should be clearly indicated. For example, instead of “Go-kul,” use “Google.”
(7) Pashto possesses a distinctive poetic rhythm and style. For example, “Ro” and “Fanah.”
190.left:
August 1939

A journey is not a hardship or a burden. In this room, I contemplate and enjoy the company of the garden. I am alone, and the scent of the roses fills the air.  I sit with a friend, sharing a cup of tea.  We discussed the impending storm, and the anxieties it would bring.  The Pashto people, particularly those from the lower regions, are deeply affected by these concerns.  The letters of the Pashto language are often neglected, and the need for a clear, standardized script is evident.  It is a matter of great importance to ensure that the language is written with precision and clarity.

and the Pashtun’s clan’s status was altered. They gathered, spoke, and became enemies.
They developed a hatred for the valley’s land. The land was now divided and fragmented,
a shadow of its former self. The clan’s reputation was tarnished.
They were scattered and dispersed, becoming the focus of gossip and slander. The assembly was broken apart,
reduced to fragments. A single family was ruined. The valley’s reputation was diminished.
The people of the valley, once accustomed to their way of life, now faced hardship and despair,
as if the sun had abandoned them. It was swift and devastating.
The Pashtun’s generosity and kindness were extended to the people of the valley,
a gesture of goodwill and a desire to see them prosper. They recognized the value of the valley’s land,
a treasure far greater than any personal gain. It was a matter of honor and loyalty,
a bond that transcended mere transactions.
193.left:
34

Not in a foreign land, but in our own. One property was sold to another,
leading to the construction of a new palace. I grew weary of seeing such transactions.
A friend’s friendship was not always a source of joy. Goodbyes were exchanged,
and friendships dissolved. The assembly was disrupted, and its purpose lost.
It was as if a great tragedy had occurred, a wound that could not be healed. (Poem)

July 11, 1939 38
A Few Pieces from the Sweet Pieces of the Homeland
(From the esteemed Mir Mumtaz Khan Sahib)

An Address to the Homeland

Sweet homeland, to be under the authority of a foreign nation, in a state of degradation, and amongst friends, this is indeed ignominy.
Sweet homeland, I see your heart weeping.
I am astonished that your people are held captive in a state of destitution.
Sweet homeland, in your abundance, you are prey in a hunting ground.
If you do not believe this, then behold this divine predicament.
Sweet homeland, perhaps this helplessness of yours is a matter of divine will.
However, your own people tell me that this is a divine affair.
Sweet homeland, love is a part of your faith.
This is a saying from the Pure Prophet to the Muslim.
Sweet homeland, may a portion of your ruined state remain.
That which is expressed in the drama of the sister's life.
Sweet homeland, your actions are indeed sweet, if this is so.
(From the pen of the esteemed Abdullah Sahib Sharifi)

195.right:
38

A few fragments of the homeland’s sorrowful memories – a poignant reminder of its hardships – are now offered.

Addressing the Homeland

Dear homeland, I observe with profound sadness how your spirit, once cherished, now lies in the hands of strangers and those who offer only fleeting friendship. I lament the bewilderment and dismay I feel witnessing the disgrace of our people.  The homeland, hunted and exposed in the wilderness, seems to wither beneath the gaze of an indifferent sky. If this uncertainty weighs heavily upon you, consider this reflection from God.  Perhaps this division and discord are the inevitable consequence of a world divided.  Let us then strive to unite, to overcome these divisions.  In this land, devoid of law and order, the nameless village remains a desolate place. The authority of the landowner and the Luqma Khan’s council governs the state, withholding power and relying solely on their own interests. They establish a separate law, overriding all others, to punish those who unjustly harm landowners, blacksmiths, and others – ordering the executioner to kill if a life is taken, and if the crime is proven, the murderer is appointed as judge.  A fine of five hundred rupees is levied upon the perpetrator, to be paid to the deceased’s family, ensuring they are not left destitute.

(From the pen of Mr. Abdullah Sahib Sharifi)

39

The Three-Month Meeting

(1)
Each village in a country possesses its own distinct customs, varying according to the desires of its inhabitants. Some prioritize their own nation, others the development of Islam and religion, and still others, their personal advantages.  A unified world is an impossibility; this division and disagreement are the result.  Therefore, it has been forced upon us.  In this lawless homeland, the nameless village remains a desolate place. The authority of the landowner and the Luqma Khan’s council governs the state, withholding power and relying solely on their own interests. They establish a separate law, overriding all others, to punish those who unjustly harm landowners, blacksmiths, and others – ordering the executioner to kill if a life is taken, and if the crime is proven, the murderer is appointed as judge.  A fine of five hundred rupees is levied upon the perpetrator, to be paid to the deceased’s family, ensuring they are not left destitute.

(2)
The present state of affairs remains suspended, awaiting the arrival of the next season and the blessings of the rains. May Muzaffar Malik find happiness in this auspicious month.

196.right:

What is the measure of grief,
What a mere drop of grapes –
I shall recall the Malik of Aden’s garden,
And feel the pangs of loss for Peshawar.
196.left:
(1)
The instructions for each village within a country’s domain are distinct and separate.
They may pursue different ambitions, sometimes favoring their own homeland, at other times Islam and the advancement of religion, and yet again, their own personal interests.
It is regrettable that the world does not operate under a single system, for this disparity and disagreement are the cause of division.
Therefore, it has been abolished in many lands,
The unruled territory of a wealthy landowner is considered a village.
The council of capitalists and the ‘Lakhma Khan’ assembly regulates the government,
Not granting authority, but rather observing it; the leader guides them,
Establishing a council for their benefit, adding a law beyond the laws of others,
Where the landowner, if he harms another, the perpetrator becomes the judge and executor, and if he harms another and proof is established, the perpetrator will be fined five hundred rupees,
And this condition is taken into account, so that no one can claim innocence in old age.
(2)
The time passes in a dreary state, the year is spent in the heat of the sun.
Sometimes there is joy – the verse of Mehrab Malik, blessed be his memory.

Pashtun Amanini
21 July 1939
Volume (19) Pashtun Amanini Number
Corresponding to 3rd Jumada al-Thani, 1358

Regarding some circles and a brief account:
A speech by Fakhr Afghan Sana

Dear brothers and sisters, I was pleased to learn that I was invited to this gathering and informed that they had expressed an intention to proceed with this matter. I offer my sincere congratulations and suggest that the President Khan consider the matter of increasing the number of God’s servants – it is a matter deserving of careful thought. However, it is important to note that those who serve God are truly servants of God, and their prayers and supplications are welcomed with joy in the community. They are a noble assembly, swift in their devotion, and we are all servants of God together. Their contributions are known, and God’s servants are greatly honored.
198.left:
Let me now present a short piece:

You will forget to shop and eat this sweet, but it is not from your own side. Like a black hunting tree, it is a burden.  It is time to take a walk and to rest, and to consider the affairs of the household.  Do not worry about wine or alcohol.  The service of God is not merely a seasonal occupation, but a constant and essential duty.  Good deeds and high morals are the foundation of true service to God.  I do not wish to be a cause of shame, and I pray that we may all find noble actions.

At the same time, I conveyed this news.

Pashtun Amanini
21 July 1939
At least you know

Let us remember that the servants of God are not merely a local phenomenon, but a universal truth.  We are all servants of God, and we should strive to live in accordance with His will.  Let us be mindful of our duty to God, and let us always seek to do good.

201.right:
The wickedness of the Nazis in France
In France, for the eighties, the Nazis are making secret efforts
They will strive until the current Prime Minister, M. Dela, becomes the Minister of War.  News from Tokyo indicates that the Japanese Minister of War of the German Government has been invited to attend the upcoming Congress in Italy, and work will be done here to provide information for Japan and the Afghans. Several German aircraft will be constructed in Italy, and the key government will discuss necessary matters and agreements with Germany.  Furthermore, Italy is aware of the burgeoning relationship between Germany and Japan, which causes unrest among the political factions in France. This unrest has become apparent only after a considerable period.  It is believed that Japan seeks to exploit the alliance with Germany, particularly in the areas of Ben Sun and other Chinese regions, where difficulties may arise.

India News
It is reported in Lahore that Pandit Jawaharlal will not be arriving from Colombo (Ceylon). He is traveling to China by plane, at the request of the Chinese government, who wish him to resolve the matter of the war with Japan and their claims regarding other kingdoms.  The Indian government also desires a similar arrangement.

India and Japan
It is reported that India and Japan are considering a commercial treaty.  Specifically, the Japanese government has yet to finalize new regulations for trade, and it is likely that Japan will act as a merchant and representative, sending their agents.

The Fate of the Bjelja Incident
News from Jahanshi reports that a flood in Kohat caused significant damage, destroying homes and electric wires.  The electric wires were torn apart and broken, and many bulls were killed and perished as a result.

Education Arrangements
The news from the Nawab states that Mr. Anzil Pir El-Bach is establishing a school in Karachi, with the aim of providing education for the railway workers in Urdu.  It is hoped that this will provide a benefit to the British and strengthen the railway system.

New Rivers in Sindh
The Minister of Public Works of Sindh has announced that new canals will be constructed in Sindh, with a portion of the budget allocated to this project.  The initial phase will begin on November 18th, and will cost 1 million rupees, with the expectation that it will bring prosperity to the region.

The German Aircraft in India
It is reported in Lahore that two military aircraft, previously hidden, have been unearthed and are now being examined.  It is said that they were concealed during the war years and were not permitted to operate.  Similar aircraft were also discovered in the areas of Bias.

Motor Vehicles in Bombay
The government of Bombay has established a motor vehicle factory.  Offers are being made to all countries for the purchase of these vehicles.

Railway Company Losses
The Indian Railway Company has suffered significant losses, estimated at 58,000 rupees and eight lakhs in the provinces of Assam during these years, compared to 508,520 rupees in the previous year.

202.right:
21 July 1929

A Prison Strike or Noobhwak Hartal
News from Patiala reports that approximately one hundred prisoners within the state’s Central Jail have initiated a hunger strike in protest of the ill-treatment by the prison officers. The exact nature of this misconduct remains unclear at present.

A Meeting of Railway Workers in India
Reports indicate that a large meeting of railway workers is scheduled for November 18th and 19th, 1929, in Bombay. This gathering will be held to consider a scheme proposed by the Chief Commissioner of Railways.

Rainfall Damage
Due to a severe flood originating from a breach in a canal near Patiala, the railway factory’s road has been affected. This has resulted in significant financial losses for the public.

Poisonous Multi-Tasking Education Management
It is reported that Nawab Anzail Pir Elhi Bakhsh Sahib, the Minister, is working to establish a college in Karachi for the purpose of education. Specifically, he intends to establish a branch of the army in the train for the Urdu language.

New Rivers in Sindh
The Minister for Public Works of Sindh has announced the commencement of new canal projects in the eastern portion of Sindh. These projects, which began in the past, will continue to be implemented in the near future.  The cost of materials for these canals will be Rs. 1 million, with a monthly price of Rs. 15,000, ensuring the land becomes fertile and productive.

German Aircraft in India
Reports from Allahabad state that two German aircraft, previously concealed, have been discovered and dismantled. These aircraft were reportedly prohibited from operating alongside those of other nations.  It is believed they were previously displayed in the city of Faizabad.

Local Cars
The government of Bombay is establishing a car factory in India, providing rejection forms to each country.

Losses of the Railway Company
The N-Blue Railway Company has incurred losses of Rs. 58,000 and seven times the amount in the provinces of Assam in recent years. In the previous year, the losses totaled Rs. 508,520.
202.left:

---
News from the Border

Opening of the Trading Factory in Troad – On July 16th, Dr. Anzibel Doctor Khan, the Prime Minister, inaugurated the Troad factory in the district of Manarand. During the occasion, the officers and nobles of the region, along with the Prime Minister, delivered brief speeches and blessings, stating that this factory, with an investment of two million rupees, would be established in the mine’s territory.

Political Conference of the Great Shaan Puli – On July 19 and 20, a great political conference was held in the district of Manarand. Under the presidency of Mr. Hussain Ahmed Sahib, Civil, the conference’s deliberations were focused on discussion and debate. The Chief of Staff, Mr. Abdul Aziz Khan Sahib, IL-A, delivered a concise speech and a powerful oration, which drew upon the history of Islam and the political affairs of the nation. The two-day conference was attended by thousands of loyal servants of God and other interested parties.

Establishment of a Hospital in Bingur – In the month of May, a large hospital was established in Bingur. The hospital, costing approximately five thousand seven hundred rupees, is fully equipped.

Meeting of Railway Workers – News reports that a large meeting of railway workers will be held on November 18 and 19, 1929, in Bombay. During this meeting, the Chief Commissioner of Railways will present a scheme for consideration.

Regarding the welfare of railway workers in India…
It is intended to provide increased benefits to railway workers in India, and to ensure that these benefits are provided. A scheme is being considered to employ military scouts and military assistants in Pakto for the purpose of protection.

Regarding the system of special management – It is reported that schemes are being considered to address any issues that may arise following the recent settlement of tribal disputes. A plan is being devised to implement a system of special management, with military scouts and military assistants stationed in Pakto for the purpose of protection.

Statement of the Mufti of Abad – On July 8th, the movement of two hundred and twenty-two prisoners from the jail of Deira Ismail Khan was ordered by the decree of the Prime Minister.

Pashtun Atmanzi
22
21 July 1939
British Policy
The British policy is to display arrogance and violence against the weak tribes, while accepting humility and humiliation in the face of powerful nations. This is a misrepresentation of reality, masking the facts of Britain’s occupation of Lin and its treaty with Severus, which led to its occupation of Abyssinia, and its secret agreement with Germany and the Soviet Union in defiance of the Hague Accords. The Allied and German joint venture in Jenin clearly revealed the deceitful nature of the British government. Following the collapse of the Republican government, the actions of Franco’s regime are merely a reflection of the same corrupt practices. Britain’s blatant disregard for the dignity of China, its open mockery of Japan, and its acceptance of every insult and display of disrespect is a shameful spectacle.

Now, instead of Japan, Great Britain seeks to establish its dominance in the regions of Mad Akhalat, attempting to conceal its own decline by accepting humiliation in exchange for honor. This is a grave error of judgment, a failure to recognize the inherent strength and independence of the Afghan people. Doubtlessly, they are the masters of power, yet no force can overcome the weakness of the oppressed. And to treat the weak with contempt is a grave injustice.

(Not Master of Mr. Amir Mumtaz Khan Sahib)

Regarding the wedding, the Japanese adopted twelve recommendations:

(1) After the marriage ceremony, I will not consider my husband’s mother as my own. Instead, she will consider you her daughter and will treat you as her own child, and in all circumstances, she will not be cruel to you.
(2) After the marriage, you must always obey your husband’s mother.
(3) Strive to ensure that your husband’s family always has a comfortable place to rest.
(4) Do not harbor any animosity towards the people of your household. This will ruin your life.
(5) If your husband speaks harshly about something, do not argue with him. Instead, try to soothe his anger.
(6) Maintain a close and intimate relationship with your husband. Do not participate in any gatherings or activities outside of his presence.
(7) Wake up early in the morning and remain vigilant until the end of the day.
(8) Manage the household carefully and maintain your accustomed routines.
(9) Do not look towards the setting sun, nor enter the homes of strangers.

Next:
(10) Reflect inwardly and observe the behavior of others.  Let your own character be evident to all.
(11) Wear simple and inexpensive clothing – maintain the dignity of your garments.
(12) Do not seek the advice of unreliable relatives or influential figures – if you are a refugee or a poor man, maintain your dignity. (From the words of Mr. Fazl Rahim, a poet from the Swat region.)

212.right:
The student’s counsel
(Pashto Master Manan School, Bannu)
1. Let the passion for Pashto be fueled by the pursuit of education.
2. Do not dwell on past mistakes; do not seek a meaningless existence.
3. True understanding requires diligence; neglecting the mind and possessions leads to long-term deprivation in this world.
4. Cultivate both knowledge and artistry; if you excel in your time, you will reap blessings in this life and the next.
5. Employ wisdom and insight in all your endeavors – this is the key to success.
6. Be literate and well-versed in your writings; this is a vital element of scholarly pursuits.
7. Embrace the habit of reading and studying fine works, thereby expanding your knowledge and artistic sensibilities.
8. Strive to maintain Pashto’s prominence in the world, safeguarding your people’s place amongst nations.
9. Now you understand what is required to advance Pashto’s education; dedicate yourself to serving your country through the light of knowledge.
10. Display your talents in all you do, and determine the path that best serves your life’s purpose.

Pashtun Atmanzi
21 July 1939

Why is Pashtun Atmanzi (Patriotism) important?

With a sense of pride, a man of Pashtun heritage inevitably asks himself: O Pashtuns! Why do you harbor such animosity, when your hearts should be filled with the warmth of friendship? You have little regard for the well-being of others. You are ever prepared to inflict harm upon those who have wronged you. Yet, the years pass without you remembering the wrongs done to you. You ignore the suffering of the young, and the plight of your own families, neglecting your wives and daughters. You do not cherish your sons or brothers. You do not value your friends or relatives. Until you treat your brother with the respect and affection he deserves, and reject the notion of disrespecting those who are innocent, you will never truly heal.  You will continue to harbor resentment and bitterness.  And after all this, you will have deprived yourselves of peace and comfort, and brought ruin upon your family.

O, owner of the steadfast heart!  Do you not see the sorrow etched upon your brother’s face?  Do you not feel a pang of shame when considering past grievances? Perhaps you have tasted the bitterness of revenge against your enemies.  But do not relish injustice.  You will not find satisfaction in retribution.  Know that other nations are progressing, sharing joys and sorrows, uniting in mutual affection and respect, forming alliances.

Pashtuns!  32

Consider the entire world; you have been driven out of Swat and beyond.  You retreated, pondered, and ultimately decided that it was necessary.

A message from the Chamberlain reached the ears of the Afghan ruler, and he considered it carefully before deciding that it was imperative to act.

The benefit lies in the Chamberlain’s appeal to the Afghan ruler, requesting that he send a message to the British government, informing them that the ruler and his people are weary of war and desire peace.  He believes that the British government will not ignore the pleas of a nation seeking tranquility and prosperity.  Night after night, Nikolia observed the situation with great concern.  The people rejoiced, and the populace flourished.  It seems the Chamberlain will sit like a king, and we will be humbled.  For what reason do we now seek to impress him?  Our representatives will return to discuss matters.  Indeed, this is the situation.  The world was astonished by the Chamberlain’s audacity.  The situation grew increasingly turbulent.  Governments began to fear the presence of foreign powers.  The Chamberlain continued his efforts to secure a lasting peace, and we were treated with disdain.  The British sent their forces to support Afghanistan against the Russians, and we were abandoned, left to face the onslaught alone.  The Afghans were forced to surrender, and the British allied themselves with Khan of Nangarhar.  The bonds of hostility were severed, and the pursuit of war ceased.  The enemy’s attacks were repulsed.  You and your forces will be cut off, and your efforts will be thwarted.  The Pashtuns, cut off from all avenues of communication, will be left to suffer.

(By the esteemed Mr. Mirmatiz Khan Sahib)

Advice to the Student
(Pashto Master Manan School, Bannu)
1. Let your passion for Pashto be fueled by the pursuit of education.
2. Do not dwell on past mistakes; do not seek a meaningless existence.
3. True understanding requires diligence; neglecting the mind and possessions leads to long-term deprivation in this world.
4. Cultivate both knowledge and artistry; if you excel in your time, you will reap blessings in this life and the next.
5. Employ wisdom and insight in all your endeavors – this is the key to success.
6. Be literate and well-versed in your writings; this is a vital element of scholarly pursuits.
7. Embrace the habit of reading and studying fine works, thereby expanding your knowledge and artistic sensibilities.
8. Strive to maintain Pashto’s prominence in the world, safeguarding your people’s place amongst nations.
9. Now you understand what is required to advance Pashto’s education; dedicate yourself to serving your country through the light of knowledge.
10. Display your talents in all you do, and determine the path that best serves your life’s purpose.

The Pashtun’s spirit is expressed through striving for excellence.  He seeks to compete with others, and he does not shy away from challenges.  Literature is not valued; a brother’s honor is his own, property is his own, and the daughter of the nation is cherished.  When suffering strikes, all feel the pain.  They sympathize with one another, offering mutual support in times of need, protecting the vulnerable.  But O Pashtun!  Do not ignore the lessons of the past.

Ward Haskam
May God bless you, the organization of education is necessary for the education of special knowledge. It is important for the end of life for the people of Adam. It is very good to agree with the program on the head of the danger of knowledge, the field of guidance. 214.right:
The attitude of Muslims towards the enemies of Islam

The history of Islam's attitude and behavior towards the enemies is preserved, what kind of attitude the Muslims had towards the enemies, and what sufferings the Muslims suffered from them, its history is recorded in a safe place. 

The holy religion of Islam, at the beginning of Islam, what kind of oppression was done by the infidels against the master of Islam, Muhammad, peace be upon him, and his companions, on the one hand, with their own lives, and against the infidels, when they were in Makkah, what kind of oppression was done on their lives in a special way, at the same time, what kind of idolatry the people against them were in, so the Prophet (PBUH) always told them about religion. As the owner of Islam in the way of Da'wah and Truth, when they were in war with the infidels and what was their attitude towards them in accordance with the commands of Allah, there is a clear example of the greatness of the holy religion of Islam and its teachings. When the Muslims in Madinah faced the infidels, they always chose their way with patience and tolerance in the face of oppression. Against the enemies, they always acted according to the teachings of Islam. Throughout the history of Islam, the attitude of the Muslims towards the enemies was such that whenever the atrocities of the enemies increased, they would stand firm against their atrocities with patience and tolerance. The stories of their patience and tolerance are preserved in the pages of history. How they treated their enemies according to the teachings of Islam was a sign of their faith and piety. In many cases, in contrast to the gentleness and humbleness of the Muslims, the cruelties and wrongdoings of the enemies have been mentioned very carefully in various parts of history, how the Muslims stood firm for the supremacy of Islam at the time of all kinds of difficulties.
214.left: During this period of the history of Islam, when they were faced with infidelity, they lived with great tolerance. As an example of enduring the oppression of the enemies at that time, when a storm of oppression was raised by the infidels against the Prophet of Islam (PBUH) and his companions, then the Muslims chose the path of patience and tolerance against it. They were engaged in the work of their da'wah despite all kinds of difficulties in explaining the word of Allah and the truth of Islam in front of them. When the oppression of the Muslims by the infidels in Makkah increased, the Prophet of Islam (PBUH) migrated from Makkah to Madinah by the order of Allah. In Madinah, there was a series of conspiracies against them by the infidels and conspiracies to eliminate them, but the followers of Islam were prepared to defend the religion of Islam against all the plots of the enemies. They were fighting with a kind of faith and courage to protect the religion of Islam in the face of all kinds of hardships from the enemies. Well, at the beginning of Islam, there are many stories of the enmity of the infidels, but the stories of the patience and tolerance of the Muslims are even more. A conspiracy group of the enemies of Islam in Madinah led by Abdullah bin Abi bin Salul, they always tried to create a crack in the ranks of Islam, but the Muslims chose the path of unity and unity against the conspiracies of the enemies, and their conspiracies failed with great patience.

222.right:
Arakat

Reflect upon yourself and your community.

Each of you should dedicate a portion of your nights to the service of God. Some among you harbor the misguided belief that God’s favor rests solely with you. As I have previously stated, our efforts to serve God in this way have yet to bear fruit.

Listen closely, friends! You will recall that in 1939, as we all faced hardship together, the telephone lines were strained and preparations were underway. The entire country and all its people were united, driven by our faith and trust in God’s servant. We held firm to our convictions. Let us now strive to strengthen these convictions and work diligently for the betterment of the nation. However, today, a troubling absence of love is evident, and a lack of unity. Why is this so? Because we, too, have turned away from God’s service.

The situation of God’s servants today is perplexing. It is as if they have lost their way, as if they are adrift. It is as if they are seeking solace in fleeting pleasures and worldly desires. It is as if they are unable to find comfort in God’s presence.  Why should God abandon us, who have turned away from His service? Why should He inflict suffering upon us, when we have forsaken His guidance?  And why do we, burdened by hardship, continue to bear the weight of misfortune?  Let us remember that true faith is not merely a fleeting thought, but a steadfast commitment.

Nine August 1939

9

...and do not seek to impose your rights upon others. My meaning is this: if you do not desire to fulfill your duties, or if you are unwilling to act with compassion, then you are not fit to serve as ministers. My role is to serve you, the minister. And if not, then I have dedicated my life to this service, for the sake of God. Therefore, wherever you find opportunity, strive to serve others.  It is not appropriate for you to seek positions of power or influence, nor should you expect more from your ministers than they are capable of providing.  Let your decisions be guided by wisdom and patience.  The mistakes of the past should serve as a lesson, not a cause for complaint.  Do not dwell on errors, but rather focus on improvement.

This is the essence of the matter.  In the police force, maintain harmony and mutual respect among all.  Avoid unnecessary disputes and disagreements.  The excessive demands of your superiors will only lead to further unrest.  Therefore, let your decisions be guided by fairness and justice.  The minister’s judgment is final, and you should accept it with humility and patience.  Let not petty grievances cloud your judgment.

Curse those who are unable to understand the situation.  I have expressed my concerns to you, my servants, that if you wish to restore the current state of affairs, you must recognize your own limitations and refrain from placing undue expectations on your ministers.  Do not seek to burden them with excessive demands.  If you find that your ministers are not suitable for the task, then you should not seek to replace them.  Let your actions be guided by prudence and wisdom.

Finally, I wish to convey this message: I will serve you as a servant of God. If you find yourself in need of assistance, you must seek it from the path of Pashtun service to God.  Do not seek to serve your own people alone.  Serve your country and your nation for the sake of God.  August 19, 1939

227.right:
Frontier News

A Millennium of Years: Pir Shah Sahib, a hundred and ten silver coins, convened a Jirga of one thousand and a loan of two hundred and seventy thousand from Kabul Khan Sahib, arrived in Peshawar on the twenty-ninth of July and returned to the twenty-seventh of July from Abbottabad.

Election in Kabul: The district of Abdul Majeed Khan had no assembly present. The Congress has addressed Abdul Majeed Khan Sahib, and his letter has been received. Mr. May Khan is his lawyer and has been appointed.

Mirdad Khan’s Absence: Mirdad Khan Sahib, currently absent from America, has engaged in some questionable service. However, the Punjab government has placed him under house arrest for two months, and he is currently receiving no payment.

Gandhi’s Retirement: On the twenty-seventh of July, Mata Gandhi departed from Abbottabad for the army.

Doctor Khan’s Leave: Recent reports have circulated falsely claiming that Dr. Khan Sahib is taking leave. This is inaccurate.

Pashtun Name Approval: The Pashtun name of Kabul has been formally approved for this month’s payment.

19
18
1 August 1931

Today’s World

The World’s Dark Road: The world is steeped in sorrow, yet hope remains. This day marks the key to the world’s affairs, a turning point after the recent upheaval of the Silver Revolution. Mussolini, emboldened by his successes in Italy, has issued a declaration to the British Empire, seeking support for a role in the coming conflict. News reports indicate that the American President has declared a policy of intervention in the war, though the precise nature of this commitment remains unclear.

In London, the news is dominated by reports of the ongoing crisis. The city is gripped by anxieties regarding the potential for aerial bombardment, with the rooftops of buildings now covered in sand to protect against the threat of falling debris.

The Anglo-Japanese Agreement: London reports that Japanese Foreign Minister Katayama is reassuring the British that relations between Japan and Britain remain amicable.

German Population Concerns: There are growing concerns regarding the German population, with speculation about their preparedness for war. It is uncertain what role the German people will play, though they appear to be mobilizing.

228.right:
Pashtun Aganzi

August 1939

The world mourned the dead, yet dreamt of better days. Let us see what fate holds in store. This day marks the turning point for the Jirga in Chalam Khawasal – a pivotal moment for the world. Following the events in Spain, Mussolini spoke of grand designs, venturing into the realms of Britain’s diminished power. He spread rumors of his ambitions, fueled by a misguided belief in his own strength. The British government, wary of Mussolini’s influence, imprisoned him for months, a testament to their apprehension.

News arrived that Dr. Khan was on leave, departing from Abatabad for Ard. However, reports circulated falsely claiming Dr. Khan was on leave due to illness.  The Pashtun pen celebrated the recognition of Khan Sahib’s name in the Kabul press. This recognition was expected to bring prestige and respect for the duration of the publication.

Mirdad Khan of the Mula region, a prominent figure, had been residing in America, and was unjustly detained by the Punjab government for two months.  His situation remained unresolved, causing considerable concern.

The situation in Abatabad remained tense.  The government’s decisions were causing confusion and hindering progress.  It was crucial to avoid repeating past mistakes and to ensure sound counsel within the government.  The unrest in the region, stemming from the discontent of the populace, demanded careful consideration.  The British authorities, observing this turmoil, sought to maintain order and stability.
228.left:
The Russian world stirred with unease.  Let us not be deceived by the rhetoric of the powerful.  The rise of Islam in Chhil Pahn signifies a shift in the times.  Consider this: if you seek solace, turn inward, towards your own heart.  However, the news reached us that the current year is also marked by the decline of the old order.  The Czechs and Poles are poised to join forces, while Turkey, Romania, and France are contemplating a unified front.  The British Parliament, observing this gathering of nations, hesitated to fully embrace it, wary of repeating past errors.  The truth is that both Mussolini and Hitler are driven by similar ambitions.  Chamberlain, the British Foreign Secretary, is increasingly seen as a puppet of Nietzsche, absorbing the philosophies of the German intellectual.  Art finds little resonance with this approach.  General Franco, a figure of strength in Spain, has solidified Germany’s position there.  Therefore, the decline of British ethics, its moral decay and political instability, is evident.  The mistakes of the past have led to a decline in the quality of governance and a loss of public trust.  It is imperative to learn from these errors and avoid repetition.  The situation in the region is further complicated by the unrest among the populace, fueled by grievances and a lack of opportunity.  The Russian influence, a constant threat, seeks to exploit these divisions.

235.right: The people of Peshawar declare their desire for freedom. They believe that the moral standards of their teachers and elders are paramount, and that these principles should guide their actions. They urge the government to recognize the importance of education and to support schools, ensuring that future generations receive a proper education.  They express a hope that the government will prioritize the welfare of the people and strive for a just and prosperous future, recognizing the need to address the grievances of the populace and to foster unity and stability within the nation.  They pray that freedom will be established in India, and that figures like Maulana Abdul Mannan, who champion the cause of liberty, will continue to inspire the people.  This declaration, issued on August 1939, reaffirms the commitment of the people of Peshawar to the pursuit of freedom and justice.

For advertisements in Pashtun, the rates are: Four rupees and six annas per year, or one rupee and two annas per issue.  For foreign publications, the rate is two and two nights per issue.

An example and account of the esteemed Hakeem Abdul Khaliq, author of “Shahid Sakina” and “Khushi Dandan,” is presented here. This section details the collaborative efforts of the four checkpoints, uniting in a shared endeavor. It highlights the importance of mutual support and the restoration of harmony between men and women.  The methods for assisting women and men in need are outlined, reflecting the Pashtun tradition of aid and assistance.

Price: 2 identifiers per copy. Price: 2 identifiers per copy.

The administration’s cable can be obtained at two locations. The Manager of the Pashtun Literary Kit, Khaliq Manzil, resides in Sharia Shahr, Khalum, Peshawar.
